TEST: Specific legal authority for this entry?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Dwelling = where someone resides. Includes flats, houses, mobile
homes, room within shared accommodation. NOT — common areas of apartment block, business
premises. When in doubt: warrant or consent.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Damache v DPP; Article 8 ECHR; Criminal Law (Defence and
Dwelling) Act 2011.

11.2 The Proportionality Test
Heaney v Ireland [1996] 1 IR 580 (SC)
FACTS: (See Interview section.) Constitutional challenge to s.52 OASA.
PRINCIPLE: PROPORTIONALITY TEST for restrictions on constitutional rights: (a) rational
connection to objective; (b) impair right as little as possible; (c) effect on right proportionate to
objective.
RATIO: Restrictions on rights must satisfy proportionality.
TEST: Rational connection + minimum impairment + proportionate effect?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Every coercive power application is a proportionality decision in
microcosm. Use the least intrusive power that achieves the policing purpose. Document the
reasoning at the time.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.3; ECHR Article 8.

11.3 ECHR — The Key Articles
Article 5 ECHR Right to liberty and security
FACTS: Liberty protection paralleling Article 40.4.1 Bunreacht. Specific grounds for lawful
detention enumerated. Procedural requirements for detention (information, judicial review,
compensation for unlawful detention).
PRINCIPLE: Detention must fall within enumerated grounds and follow procedure prescribed by
law. Suspect must be informed promptly of reasons. Promptly brought before judge for serious
offences. Right to challenge legality.
TEST: Detention within enumerated ground + procedure prescribed by law + safeguards?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Reasons given at arrest (Christie v Leachinsky). Detention within
statutory power. Charge or release within time limit. Bail decisions reviewable.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.4.1; ECHR Act 2003.
Article 6 ECHR Right to fair trial
FACTS: Fair trial protection paralleling Article 38.1 Bunreacht. Specific rights: presumption of
innocence, information of accusation, time and facilities for defence, legal representation,
examination of witnesses, interpreter.
PRINCIPLE: Trial must be fair, in reasonable time, by independent tribunal. Specific rights
enumerated.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Every aspect of investigation feeds Article 6. Disclosure,
interpreter, legal representation, evidence quality, time limits.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 38.1; Saunders v UK; Murray v UK; ECHR Act 2003.
Article 8 ECHR Right to private and family life
FACTS: Privacy protection paralleling Article 40.3 unenumerated rights. Interference must be in
accordance with law, pursue legitimate aim, be necessary in democratic society.
PRINCIPLE: Privacy is qualified right — interference requires authorisation. Legitimate aim.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Necessity.
TEST: Authorised + legitimate aim + necessary + proportionate?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Phone searches, social media examination, surveillance, data
extraction — all engage Article 8. Each requires authorisation (warrant, court order, statutory power,
consent). Proportionality reasoning.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.3; Damache; Surveillance Act 2009.
ECHR Act 2003 Domesticating the Convention
FACTS: Convention rights given further effect in Irish law.
PRINCIPLE: Courts to interpret statutes in compliance with Convention. Damages available for
breach.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Every coercive act assessable against ECHR. Document
proportionality reasoning.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Articles 5, 6, 8 ECHR.

11.4 Specific Constitutional Cases
State (Healy) v Donoghue [1976] IR 325 (SC)
FACTS: Accused without means refused legal aid for serious case; conviction challenged.
PRINCIPLE: Where charge is serious and accused lacks means, legal representation is
constitutionally required.
RATIO: Legal aid is constitutional right for serious cases involving impecunious accused.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: At first court appearance: ensure accused informed of legal aid
eligibility. Solicitor list available. Custody Sgt verifies.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 38.1; Article 6 ECHR.
O'Callaghan v AG / Bail Act 1997 [1966] IR 501 / Statute
FACTS: Bail principles; refusal grounds. O'Callaghan originally limited preventive bail refusal;
modified by 16th Amendment 1996 and Bail Act 1997.
PRINCIPLE: Bail engages presumption of innocence. Statutory grounds for refusal include:
seriousness + likelihood of further offences (4+ year offences), flight risk, witness interference,
victim protection.
TEST: Statutory grounds for objection met?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Bail objection statement structure: serious offence + antecedents
demonstrating pattern + current charges + drug/alcohol/mental health factors + victim concerns.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Bail Act 1997 s.2; bail line.
DPP v Carmody [2010] 1 IR 635 (SC)
FACTS: Procedural fairness in summary proceedings; legal aid issue.
PRINCIPLE: Procedural fairness applies across criminal proceedings — informed of charge, time
to prepare, legal representation, disclosure proportionate.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Summary file preparation: charges clear, disclosure to defence
(short brief typically), witness availability. Don't treat District Court as procedural shortcut.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 38.1; Healy v Donoghue.
DPP v Damache [2012] IESC 11
FACTS: (See Search section.) S.29 OASA warrant by Garda Super — unconstitutional.
PRINCIPLE: Search warrants for dwelling must be issued by independent judicial figure.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Dwelling warrants — DJ. Document independence.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.5; Article 8 ECHR; warrant procedures.
DPP v Curtin [2006] 2 IR 366
FACTS: Privacy challenge to handling of communications evidence.
PRINCIPLE: Communications privacy strongly protected; interception, retention, examination
require lawful authorisation.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Phone evidence within statutory framework. Retention for lawful
purpose only. Don't browse seized phones — CCU within authorisation.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.3; Article 8 ECHR; 1993 Act.
McD v L [2009] IESC 81
FACTS: Privacy in family law context — but illustrates principles.
PRINCIPLE: Privacy constitutionally and ECHR-protected. Interference must be in accordance
with law, pursue legitimate aim, be necessary in democratic society.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Article 40.3; Article 8 ECHR; surveillance authorisations.

12. Procedure — Charging, DPP, Court Process
How the file goes from station to court. CM13, Book of Evidence, DPP directions, witness preparation, court
testimony. The mid-level Garda is the file architect.
Procedure — DPP, file preparation, court process, charge selection, disclosure schedule, GSOC. The mid-level
Garda builds the file the DPP needs to make principled prosecutorial decisions. Charge what the evidence
supports, not what would be desired. The file is the case in the District Court; in the higher courts, the Book of
Evidence and trial preparation become central.

12.1 The DPP
Director of Public Prosecutions Act 1974 Statutory framework
FACTS: DPP established as independent prosecutorial authority. Decisions independent of
executive and investigating force. DPP exercises functions in public interest.
PRINCIPLE: Constitutional separation of investigation from prosecutorial decision.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Investigation file → DPP directions. Don't pre-empt DPP
decisions. Recommendations may be sought but DPP decides.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP Guidelines.
DPP Guidelines (Current Edition) Prosecutorial test
FACTS: Two-stage test: (1) Evidential test — reasonable prospect of conviction? (2) Public interest
test — is prosecution in public interest?
PRINCIPLE: Prosecution is principled exercise — not automatic.
TEST: Both stages satisfied?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: File preparation: present evidence completely, identify
weaknesses honestly, address foreseeable defences. Public interest factors documented where they
apply.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP Act 1974; DPP v Sweetman.
DPP v Sweetman [2009] IESC 51
FACTS: Public interest factors in prosecution decision.
PRINCIPLE: DPP weighs evidential and public interest factors. Courts respect prosecutorial
discretion within constitutional limits.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Build file to enable principled discretion: full evidence picture,
victim impact, accused antecedents, diversion possibilities, complexity.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP Guidelines.

12.2 Charge File and CM13
CM13 (Charge Sheet) and CCRMS Practical framework
FACTS: CM13 — formal charge document. CCRMS — case and crime recording management
system. Both feed into book of evidence and court process.
PRINCIPLE: Standardised documentation.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: CM13 completion: (a) charge precisely worded with section/Act
and date; (b) statement of facts — concise narrative; (c) evidence list — statements, exhibits,
interview, identifications; (d) accused particulars — verified; (e) signed by investigating member.
Errors propagate — wrong charge wording = case at risk.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Wrong section cited. (2) Wrong date of offence. (3) Accused
particulars unverified. (4) Evidence list incomplete.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP file requirements.
Charge Selection — DPP v Ó Súilleabháin [1995] 2 IR 317
FACTS: Charge selection issue.
PRINCIPLE: Charge what the evidence supports, not what would be desired. Overcharging
weakens credibility; undercharging fails victim and public interest.
TEST: Does the evidence support the elements of the charge selected?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Charge selection: identify all available charges, assess evidence
against elements, select what evidence supports. Common errors: burglary where only theft (no
trespasser entry), s.3 NFOAPA where only s.2 (harm threshold not met), robbery where force not at
time of theft.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Individual offence statutes.

12.3 Book of Evidence and Trial Process
Criminal Procedure Act 1967 — Book of Evidence Statutory framework
FACTS: Service of Book of Evidence on accused for indictable offences. Book contains statements,
exhibits, list of witnesses.
PRINCIPLE: Pre-trial disclosure of prosecution case.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Book preparation: every statement, every exhibit, list of all
witnesses. Quality control — typos, factual errors, inconsistencies addressed before service.
Statements signed and dated. Cross-references correct.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP directions; pre-trial procedure.
S.4A-K CPA 1967 — Pre-trial procedure Statutory framework
FACTS: Modern pre-trial procedure — applications for dismissal, addition of witnesses, additional
evidence, severance, joinder.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Pre-trial preparation: meet prosecutor, address anticipated
defence applications, prepare witnesses, schedule, manage exhibits. Witness availability confirmed.
Vulnerable witness measures applied for.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Trial process.

12.4 Special Procedures
Special Criminal Court Offences Against the State Act 1939 / s.7 CJ(A)A 2009

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

FACTS: SCC trial: three-judge panel, no jury, more formalised. Used principally for OC offences
where jury intimidation risk.
PRINCIPLE: Preserves trial process integrity where ordinary court process compromised.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Investigation file to higher disclosure standard. Witness
intimidation considerations — anonymity, special measures, s.16 sworn statements at outset. Brief
witnesses on SCC process.
CROSS-REFERENCES: OC offences; s.16 CJA 2006.
Criminal Procedure Act 2010 — Double jeopardy Statutory framework
FACTS: Allows retrial in serious cases where compelling new evidence emerges or acquittal
tainted.
PRINCIPLE: Modifies common law double jeopardy where necessary to prevent injustice.
TEST: (1) Eligible (serious offence)? (2) Compelling new evidence? (3) Retrial in justice?
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Cold case reviews. Application via DPP to Court of Appeal.
Investigation properly resourced — file located, evidence retrieved, witnesses re-traced.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Cold case investigation.

12.5 Garda Accountability
Garda Síochána Act 2005 (as amended) Statutory framework
FACTS: Functions: preserve peace, protect life/property, prevent/detect crime, prosecute. Operates
under Policing Authority; accountable to GSOC, Inspectorate.
PRINCIPLE: Modern statutory framework. Multi-layered oversight.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Anchor your actions in statutory duty. Court testimony: 'In the
course of my duty under s.7 of the Garda Síochána Act 2005 of preventing crime, I observed...'
GSOC referrals: serious matters automatically referable.
CROSS-REFERENCES: GSOC Act; Policing Authority.
Garda Síochána (Discipline) Regulations 2007 Internal regime
FACTS: Internal disciplinary regime — investigations by appropriate rank, breach categories,
procedural safeguards.
PRINCIPLE: Internal accountability.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Conduct yourself as if every action is reviewable. PULSE entries
factual and contemporaneous. Use of force reports promptly. Custody Regs adherence.
CROSS-REFERENCES: GSOC; Code of Ethics.
GSOC — Garda Síochána Ombudsman Commission Statutory body
FACTS: Independent oversight of Garda complaints and serious incidents.
PRINCIPLE: Specific categories automatically referred (death/serious injury in custody, discharge
of firearm, etc).
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Where GSOC investigation, cooperate fully. Statement provided
through line management. Don't discuss case with colleagues during GSOC process.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Garda Síochána Act 2005.
VOLUME 5
WITNESS HANDLING
Statement Quality, Vulnerable Witnesses, Hostile Witnesses, Experts

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

13. Witnesses — How They Work and Unfold in Court
Witnesses are the case. Statement quality, vulnerable witness measures, hostile witness procedure, expert
witnesses, court preparation. A great investigation can collapse on poor witness work.
Witnesses are the case. The Garda is the conduit between what witnesses saw and what the court hears.
Statement quality, witness preparation, vulnerable witness measures, hostile witness procedure, expert
witnesses — each demands specific handling. A great investigation can collapse on poor witness work; a
difficult case can succeed on good witness work.

9.1 Statement-Taking — The Foundation
Statement Quality — The Core Discipline Best practice
FACTS: Every prosecution rests on witness statements. The quality of a statement at the time of
taking determines its value at trial — often years later.
PRINCIPLE: Statements should: (a) be in the witness's own words; (b) cover all matters the witness
can speak to; (c) be made in conditions free from contamination; (d) be signed and dated; (e) include
all sensory details (what saw, heard, smelled, felt); (f) capture verbatim words spoken by
suspect/others where significant; (g) be timed accurately (when taken, when events occurred).
TEST: Can a barrister read this statement in three years' time and call this witness with confidence?
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence cross-examines on the statement word-for-word.
Discrepancies between statement and trial testimony are the standard attack. The Garda taking the
statement may also be cross-examined on circumstances of taking.
SPECIMEN TESTIMONY: (Garda evidence on statement-taking): "At [time] at [location], in
conditions [private room, free of distraction, no other parties present except [if any]], I took a
statement from [witness]. The statement was taken in writing. I asked open questions and recorded
the witness's account in their own words to the greatest extent possible. After completion, the
statement was read back to the witness, who confirmed its accuracy and signed each page in my
presence."
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Conditions: private space, no suspect/co-witnesses present, no
time pressure. Open questions: 'tell me what happened', 'describe what you saw', 'what did the person
look like?'. Avoid leading on key facts. Record verbatim where significant ('he said: 'I'll fucking kill
you', not 'he made a threat'). Read back to witness, allow corrections, sign each page. Date and time
of taking.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Statement signed and dated. Notebook entry on
circumstances of taking. Witness's contact details verified.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Leading questions on key facts. (2) Paraphrasing witness's words. (3)
Taking statement in suspect's presence. (4) Witnesses giving statements in each other's presence
(cross-contamination). (5) Single sweep — not returning for follow-up where matters emerge. (6)
Failure to sign each page.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Yates (hostile witness); s.16 CJA 2006 (sworn statements); DPP
v Tuite.
Section 16, Criminal Justice Act 2006 — Sworn Statement Discipline Statutory framework
FACTS: (See also Evidence section.) Sworn statement procedure designed to support admissibility
where witness later retracts or becomes hostile.
PRINCIPLE: A sworn statement made before a peace commissioner, in circumstances of
voluntariness and reliability, may be admitted as substantive evidence where the witness at trial
refuses to give evidence, gives materially inconsistent evidence, or denies making the prior
statement.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: DV cases especially: take sworn statement (s.16 format) at first
opportunity. Peace commissioner administers oath. Statement covers all material matters.
Conditions documented — private, no suspect present, no intoxication, no pressure. Where there is

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

any reason to anticipate later retraction (intimate partner, gang witness, vulnerable), s.16 is the
protection. The statement must be reliable enough on its own — comprehensive, voluntary, accurate.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Sworn statement signed and dated, peace commissioner
attestation. Notebook entry on circumstances.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Cursory s.16 statement that wouldn't stand alone. (2) Statement taken
in unsuitable conditions. (3) Failure to take s.16 statement when circumstances clearly warrant.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Yates; hostile witness procedure; DV practice.

9.2 Vulnerable Witnesses
Criminal Evidence Act 1992 — Special Measures Statutory framework
FACTS: Provides special measures for vulnerable witnesses: video link, screens, intermediary,
removal of formal court dress, written statements as evidence in chief.
PRINCIPLE: Where giving evidence in open court would cause significant distress or impair
quality of evidence, special measures available to enable best evidence. Application to court for
measures; threshold varies.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Identify vulnerability early — at statement-taking stage.
Document on file. Special measures application via prosecutor in advance of trial. Coordinate with
court services for video link / screens. Pre-trial visit for witness to court familiarisation.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Statement on vulnerability factors. Medical /
psychological evidence where applicable. Prosecutor's application.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Late identification of vulnerability. (2) Failure to apply for measures.
(3) Failure to prepare witness for special measures setting.
CROSS-REFERENCES: CEA 1992; DV practice; child witness protocols.
Child Witnesses — Specialist Interview Specialist Interviewer Unit protocols
FACTS: Child complainants in sexual / abuse cases interviewed by specialist trained interviewers,
video-recorded. The recording typically serves as evidence in chief.
PRINCIPLE: Specialist interview minimises trauma, uses age-appropriate techniques, produces
reliable evidence. Frontline Gardaí handle the initial complaint and refer to specialist unit.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: First Garda role on child complaint: (a) ensure immediate safety
and welfare; (b) Tusla referral (Children First Act 2015); (c) preserve scene / evidence; (d) take
initial account in age-appropriate language (open questions only, brief, document verbatim); (e) refer
to specialist unit for evidential interview; (f) avoid leading questions or repeated questioning that
may contaminate recall.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Initial account in notebook (verbatim, brief). Tusla
referral form. Referral to specialist unit. Specialist interview video.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Detailed initial questioning that contaminates specialist interview. (2)
Leading questions to child. (3) Multiple Gardaí taking initial accounts. (4) Delay in specialist
referral.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Children First Act 2015; CEA 1992; CL(SO)A 2017.
Sexual Offence Complainants Specialist protocols
FACTS: Adult sexual offence complainants benefit from specialist victim liaison, medical
examination, safety planning, and supportive trial preparation.
PRINCIPLE: Trauma-informed approach. Avoid re-traumatisation through repeated accounts.
Preserve forensic opportunities urgently. Victim choice on procedure.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: First Garda: scene preservation, immediate welfare, prompt
medical examination (Sexual Assault Treatment Unit), preserve clothing and forensic samples.
Specialist victim liaison Garda involved. Statement-taking by trained personnel where possible. Use
of CEA 1992 special measures for trial.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Multiple statement-takings. (2) Pressuring victim through process. (3)
Failure to preserve forensic opportunities. (4) Treating complaint as not credible based on victim's
emotional state.
CROSS-REFERENCES: CL(SO)A 2017; Sex Offenders Act 2001; CEA 1992; Children First Act
2015.

9.3 Hostile and Reluctant Witnesses
DPP v Yates [2010] IECCA
FACTS: (Already covered in Evidence section.) Witness inconsistent at trial; hostile witness
procedure.
PRINCIPLE: Where witness materially inconsistent at trial, court may rule hostile, allowing crossexamination by calling party. Prior s.16 statement admissible under conditions.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Anticipate hostility for: intimate partner victims, gang-associated
witnesses, vulnerable witnesses, witnesses subject to intimidation. Take s.16 statement at outset.
Multiple statement sessions where appropriate. Identify supporting evidence independent of witness
account.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: s.16 statement; supporting independent evidence;
statements from other witnesses; CCTV; forensic.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Single statement assumption. (2) Heavy reliance on single witness
without corroboration. (3) Failure to anticipate retraction.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.16 CJA 2006; DV practice; witness intimidation considerations.
Witness Intimidation — s.41 CJA 1999 Statutory framework
FACTS: Section 41 CJA 1999 criminalises intimidation or harm to witnesses in criminal
proceedings.
PRINCIPLE: A person who, with intent to obstruct/pervert course of justice in criminal
proceedings: harms or threatens to harm a witness; intimidates a witness; commits offence. Up to 15
years.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Inner-city OC cases: witness intimidation is a primary risk.
Document any approach, threat, or attempt. Witness's statement on intimidation forms basis of
separate s.41 charge AND application for special measures / protection. PULSE alerts. Liaison with
Witness Protection where appropriate.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Treating witness intimidation as 'just talk'. (2) Failure to charge
intimidation separately. (3) Inadequate witness protection planning.
CROSS-REFERENCES: CEA 1992 (measures); CJA 1999 s.41; SCC certification.

9.4 Expert Witnesses
Expert Witness Management Best practice + DPP v Buckley
FACTS: Expert witnesses produce specialist reports and give testimony on technical matters.
PRINCIPLE: (See DPP v Buckley in Evidence section.) Expert qualifications, methodology, basis
must be transparent. Defence may engage expert in opposition.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Common experts: FSI (drugs, DNA, ballistics, fibres,
fingerprints), cell-site, accident reconstruction, forensic accountant, medical (consultant grade for
serious cases), IT/CCU, scenes of crime examiner. Brief expert with full case context. Disclose
report in advance. Anticipate defence expert. Prepare for cross-examination of own expert.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Expert's CV, methodology, data underlying opinion.
Disclosure timetable.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Briefing only with prosecution-favourable material. (2) Withholding
underlying data. (3) Expert testifying beyond field. (4) Late instruction.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Buckley; DPP v JF; DPP v Smyth.

9.5 Witness Preparation for Court
Court Preparation — Lay Witnesses Best practice
FACTS: Lay witnesses (members of the public, victims) often have no prior court experience.
Quality of evidence often depends on preparation.
PRINCIPLE: Witnesses entitled to court familiarisation, expectation-setting, support. Coaching on
substantive evidence is improper. Procedural orientation is proper.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Pre-trial: re-read statement with witness, identify likely crossexamination areas (without coaching on answers), explain court layout, explain procedure (oath,
examination-in-chief, cross, re-examination), arrange transport / parking, ensure availability. For
vulnerable: pre-trial court visit, screens / video link confirmed.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Witness liaison log. Pre-trial contact entries. Vulnerable
witness measures confirmed.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) No preparation at all (witness arrives cold). (2) Coaching on substance
(improper). (3) Failure to confirm availability. (4) Not arranging vulnerable measures.
CROSS-REFERENCES: CEA 1992; Court Service victim support.
Court Preparation — Garda Witnesses Best practice
FACTS: Gardaí giving evidence are also witnesses, subject to all the same standards plus the
heightened scrutiny that comes from professional involvement.
PRINCIPLE: Garda evidence anchored in notebook and statement. Re-familiarise before trial.
Brief with prosecutor. Identify likely defence lines.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: (See Constitutional & Procedure section on Court Testimony.)
Re-read notebook entries, statement, exhibits list. Walk through chronology. Identify procedural
points likely to be cross-examined (arrest grounds, caution administration, detention extensions,
search conduct). Brief with prosecutor. Anticipate JC analysis points if any constitutional or
procedural breach in the case.
WITNESS / EVIDENCE HANDLING: Pre-trial brief notes. Statement and notebook to hand.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Going to court without re-reading the statement. (2) Memorising
answers (sounds rehearsed). (3) Speculating beyond evidence. (4) Confrontational tone with defence
counsel.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Tuite; Garda Síochána Act 2005.

9.6 Witness Common Pitfalls — Cross-Cutting
Recurring witness-related problems:
Cross-contamination at scene (recurring)
FACTS: Multiple witnesses to incident allowed to discuss before separate statements taken.
PRINCIPLE: Statements should be free of contamination by other accounts.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: At scene: separate witnesses physically. Take details, then
separate statement-taking. If statements at scene impractical, instruct witnesses not to discuss and
arrange follow-up separately.
COMMON PITFALLS: (1) Witnesses sitting together. (2) Witness already informed of another's
account.
Verbal vs written statements (recurring)
FACTS: Witness gives verbal account at scene; written statement taken later differs.
PRINCIPLE: Verbal initial account is potentially evidence but written statement is the document.
Inconsistencies are defence material.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Note verbal account in notebook verbatim where possible. When
taking formal written statement, include the witness's full account, not selected aspects. If written
statement differs from verbal account, address openly with witness.
Late statement-taking (recurring)
FACTS: Statement not taken until days/weeks after incident.
PRINCIPLE: Memory degrades. Statements taken promptly are stronger evidence.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Statement at scene where possible. Within 24 hours certainly for
serious matters. Where delay unavoidable, document the reason.
Failure to follow up with witnesses (recurring)
FACTS: Initial statement taken; subsequent investigation reveals new matters witness can speak to;
no follow-up statement.
PRINCIPLE: Witness statements may need supplementation as investigation develops.
PRACTICAL APPLICATION: Witness liaison maintained. Where new investigative material
connects to witness, prompt follow-up statement.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Continuing disclosure obligation (DPP v Wilson).
VOLUME 6
COMMON MISTAKES
What Actually Goes Wrong in Court

14. Common Mistakes That Come Up in Court
Cases fail on preventable mistakes. This catalogues recurring problems organised by where they occur in the
policing process. Each is preventable with the discipline outlined in the substantive sections; this section is
what NOT to do.
Cases don't usually fail because of bad investigation — they fail because of preventable mistakes. This section
catalogues the recurring problems that surface in case law and operational review, organised by where they
typically occur in the policing process. Each is preventable with the discipline outlined in the substantive
sections; this section is a checklist of what NOT to do.

10.1 Mistakes at Arrest
✗ Not Personally Forming Reasonable Suspicion DPP v Walsh [1980] IR 294
THE ISSUE: Arresting member acts on colleague's say-so without independent grounds.
WHY IT MATTERS: Walsh requires personal suspicion at moment of arrest. Borrowed suspicion
is not enough.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Court testimony asks: 'What did YOU know at the moment of
arrest?' If the answer is 'Sergeant told me to', the arrest is defective and downstream evidence at risk.
HOW TO PREVENT: Before arrest, articulate the grounds in one sentence: 'I am arresting because
I personally know [facts] which give reasonable grounds.' If briefing officer relayed information,
accept it on reasonable grounds AND know the substance — that becomes your own.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Walsh; Christie v Leachinsky.
✗ Wrong Section Cited at Arrest (recurring across case law)
THE ISSUE: Reflexive 'section 30' when actually s.4 CJA 1984 applies, or vice versa.
WHY IT MATTERS: Each power has specific conditions. Wrong citation may render arrest
defective if the cited power doesn't authorise.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence asks: 'Under what power did you arrest?' Confusion or
hesitation invites further cross-examination.
HOW TO PREVENT: Know the major powers cold: s.4 CJA 1984 (standard arrestable); s.30
OASA (scheduled); s.30 CJ(PO)A 1994 (public order); s.49(8) RTA (drunk driving). Notebook
entry of specific power.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Dunne v Clinton; specific arrest statutes.
✗ Failure to State Reason at Arrest Christie v Leachinsky [1947]
THE ISSUE: 'You're under arrest' without stating the offence, or stating in legal jargon.
WHY IT MATTERS: Reason must be communicated at arrest in language reasonably calculated
to be understood. Plain language required.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence: 'What exactly did you say at the doorstep?' Vague
answers undermine the case.
HOW TO PREVENT: Standard format: 'I am Garda X. I am arresting you for [plain English
offence]. You are not obliged to say anything…' Practise this cold.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Christie v Leachinsky; DPP v Pringle.
✗ Pretextual Arrest State (Trimbole) v Governor Mountjoy line
THE ISSUE: Arrest for minor matter as cover for investigation of unrelated serious matter.
WHY IT MATTERS: Arrest must be for genuine investigative purpose of the cited offence. Pretext
may amount to abuse of process.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence may show disproportionate investigative activity on
unrelated matter; internal communications may reveal pretextual intent.
HOW TO PREVENT: If you suspect involvement in serious matter, get grounds for that matter
and arrest for it. Don't use minor charges as 'ways in'.
CROSS-REFERENCES: State (Trimbole); DPP v JC; Article 40.4.1.

10.2 Mistakes in Search
✗ Executing Without Reading the Warrant DPP v Dunne [1994]
THE ISSUE: Warrant defects (wrong address, expired date, missing signature) not caught.
WHY IT MATTERS: Warrant validity is constitutional. Defects expose evidence.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence obtains copy and scrutinises. Each defect is an
admissibility point.
HOW TO PREVENT: Before crossing threshold: read the warrant. Confirm address against what
you see. Check date and signature. Photograph for file.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Dunne; Article 40.5.
✗ Wrong Premises (recurring)
THE ISSUE: Warrant for No. 7 Flat A; executed at Flat B. Or no flat specified in multi-unit
building.
WHY IT MATTERS: Premises specificity is constitutional. Wrong premises = unlawful entry.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence pinpoints discrepancy between warrant and actual
premises entered.
HOW TO PREVENT: Pause and resolve any address doubt before entry. Flats need flat / unit /
floor specified. Cross-check signage, number, door colour.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Dunne; Article 40.5.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

✗ Generic Grounds in Warrant Application (recurring)
THE ISSUE: Sworn information says 'I have reasonable grounds' without specifics.
WHY IT MATTERS: DJ may accept thin grounds at application but trial court may not on review.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence demands disclosure of warrant application. Thin
grounds undermine warrant validity.
HOW TO PREVENT: Write the information with specifics: dates, observations, intelligence basis
('confidential information from a source whose information has previously been reliable'), associated
arrests, surveillance products.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Dunne; s.26 MDA.
✗ Scope Creep — Searching Beyond Warrant (recurring)
THE ISSUE: Drug warrant becomes wide-ranging cupboard-by-cupboard search.
WHY IT MATTERS: Search scope determined by what is sought. Going further requires plain
view or further warrant.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence challenges items seized beyond scope. Plain view
exception scrutinised — was item obviously evidential? Was the searcher progressing within
original scope?
HOW TO PREVENT: Brief search team on scope. Pause and consider before opening anything
that wouldn't contain what we're looking for.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.7 CJA 2006 (plain view); DPP v Heaphy.
✗ Searching Persons Not on Premises (recurring with s.26 MDA)
THE ISSUE: Warrant authorises search of persons 'found therein'; person searched outside
premises.
WHY IT MATTERS: Authority extends only to persons on premises at execution.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence challenges the search; evidence may be excluded.
HOW TO PREVENT: Search persons on premises only. Persons outside need separate grounds
(s.23 MDA, s.30 CJ(PO)A, s.8 CJA 2006).
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.26 MDA; statutory stop-search powers.
✗ Continuity Gaps in Seized Items DPP v Bowes / Doolan line
THE ISSUE: Exhibit number changed without record; seal broken without explanation; gap
between scene and PEMS entry.
WHY IT MATTERS: Continuity is foundational. Break may render evidence inadmissible.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence cross-examines each handler. Inconsistencies between
notebook, PEMS, continuity statements exposed.
HOW TO PREVENT: Seal at scene. Sign across seal. Photograph. PEMS entry promptly. Every
transfer signed and timed. Continuity statements from each handler.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Bowes; DPP v Doolan.
✗ Browsing Seized Phone at Station DPP v Forde / Egan
THE ISSUE: Phone seized and content browsed without separate authority.
WHY IT MATTERS: Seizure incident to arrest is one act; content access requires separate
authority.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence: 'When and how did you access the phone content?'
Browsing at station without warrant exposes the content evidence.
HOW TO PREVENT: Power off, Faraday bag, CCU referral. Do NOT browse.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Forde; DPP v Egan; s.6 CJA 1984.

10.3 Mistakes in Detention
✗ Late Extension Authorisation DPP v Finn / O'Brien line
THE ISSUE: Extension that should have been at hour 6:00 signed at hour 6:30.
WHY IT MATTERS: Authorisations must be timely. Retrospective authorisation at risk of being
no authorisation.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Custody record shows the time discrepancy. Defence may argue
detention unlawful for the gap.
HOW TO PREVENT: Set alarms. Brief the Superintendent before the deadline. Build buffer.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Brien; DPP v Finn; s.4 CJA 1984.
✗ Detention Clock Suspensions Wrongly Recorded DPP v Boylan
THE ISSUE: 8 hours rest recorded but actual rest was 4 hours; solicitor consultation timed wrong;
suspect interviewed during 'rest'.
WHY IT MATTERS: Suspensions are protections for suspect. Falsified suspensions are serious
misconduct.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence cross-examines on custody record vs other records
(interview times, cell visits). Inconsistencies expose dishonest entries.
HOW TO PREVENT: Genuine rest, genuine consultation. Record actual times. Where rest
interrupted, record interruption.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Boylan; Custody Regulations 1987.
✗ Custody Record Backfilled DPP v Bowes & McGrath
THE ISSUE: Entries made retrospectively at end of shift; tidied to look better.
WHY IT MATTERS: Custody record must be contemporaneous and honest.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence forensic analysis of custody record — handwriting
changes, ink differences, suspicious blocks of entries — expose backfill.
HOW TO PREVENT: Live entries. If you missed an entry, note when you noticed and when
entered.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Bowes & McGrath; DPP v Buck.
✗ Solicitor Request Not Actioned Gormley & White v DPP
THE ISSUE: Suspect requests solicitor; interview proceeds before consultation.
WHY IT MATTERS: Constitutional right to access. Substantive questioning before consultation
is breach.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence checks custody record for request time, consultation
time, interview start time. Gap is exposed.
HOW TO PREVENT: Solicitor request = hard stop on interview. Contact promptly. Document
efforts.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Gormley & White; DPP v Doyle.
✗ Marathon Interview Without Breaks DPP v Holland / Madden
THE ISSUE: Interview sessions of 3+ hours without break; cumulative interview time excessive
without rest.
WHY IT MATTERS: Oppression by length. Voluntariness at risk.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Interview times tabulated against breaks against rest.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Insufficient rest = exclusion.
HOW TO PREVENT: 2 hours max per session. Genuine breaks (out of room, refreshment, toilet).
Document.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Holland; DPP v Madden; DPP v Ward.

10.4 Mistakes in Caution and Inference Procedure
✗ Paraphrasing the Caution DPP v Pringle
THE ISSUE: Improvising the caution wording; adding extra-statutory commentary.
WHY IT MATTERS: The wording is prescribed. Deviation undermines the caution's protective
function.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Recording captures the exact words. Variant wording is defence
point.
HOW TO PREVENT: Memorise verbatim. Practise. Don't add anything.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Pringle; DPP v Conroy.
✗ Paraphrasing the Inference Warning DPP v Connolly
THE ISSUE: Reading inference warning (s.18/19/19A CJA 1984) from memory rather than
prescribed form.
WHY IT MATTERS: Strict compliance required. Deviation invalidates the inference.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence cross-references actual words against the prescribed
form.
HOW TO PREVENT: Read verbatim from the station-issued form. Do not deviate.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Connolly; Murray v UK.
✗ Wrong Inference Warning for the Question (recurring)
THE ISSUE: s.18 warning used for a presence question (s.19 territory) or vice versa.
WHY IT MATTERS: Each section has different wording and different test.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence identifies the mismatch.
HOW TO PREVENT: Map your specific question to the correct section: physical
item/substance/mark = s.18; presence at place = s.19; defence fact = s.19A.
CROSS-REFERENCES: ss.18, 19, 19A CJA 1984.
✗ Inference Warning Without Solicitor Consultation Murray v UK / DPP v Connolly
THE ISSUE: Inference warning deployed before solicitor has been consulted.
WHY IT MATTERS: Solicitor access is the gateway safeguard. Without it, the inference is
unavailable.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Custody record shows no solicitor consultation before warning.
Inference excluded.
HOW TO PREVENT: Sequence: solicitor → warning → question. Never skip solicitor step.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Murray v UK; Gormley & White; DPP v Connolly.
✗ Subtle Inducement DPP v Tyndall
THE ISSUE: Soft inducement language: 'it would be in your interest…', 'the judge will look
favourably…', 'think of your children…'.
WHY IT MATTERS: Voluntariness requires absence of inducement, however subtle.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Recording captures the exchange. Inducement language is

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

defence material.
HOW TO PREVENT: No promises. No conditional language. Standard response to 'what happens
if I cooperate?': 'I can't promise you anything. The decision is yours. Anything you say is evidence.'
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Tyndall; DPP v Lynch; DPP v Hoey.
✗ Off-Tape Conversations Touching Substance (recurring)
THE ISSUE: Substantive conversation in transport, cell, holding area outside formal interview.
WHY IT MATTERS: Every conversation is potentially evidence. Off-tape exchanges undermine
recording protection.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Suspect alleges admissions made off-tape; Garda denies.
Recording cannot resolve. Court faces credibility contest.
HOW TO PREVENT: If suspect raises substantive matters informally: pause, return to interview
room, recommence recording, re-caution. Keep informal exchanges to non-substantive matters.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Mallon; DPP v Hoey.

10.5 Mistakes in Evidence Handling
✗ Late CCTV Recovery Braddish v DPP
THE ISSUE: CCTV not recovered until weeks after incident; footage overwritten.
WHY IT MATTERS: Preservation duty is active. Failure to seek out and preserve relevant material
is breach.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence applies for stay on basis of lost evidence. Court
considers efforts made.
HOW TO PREVENT: Within 24-72 hours of incident. Written preservation request to keeper.
Identify all potential CCTV sources within 1km of scene.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Braddish v DPP; Dunne v DPP; Ludlow v DPP.
✗ CCTV Without Keeper Statement DPP v O'Brien
THE ISSUE: CCTV exhibited without statement on system, time accuracy, retention.
WHY IT MATTERS: Authentication is foundational. Without it, CCTV at risk.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence demands authentication; thin authentication exposes
evidence.
HOW TO PREVENT: Statement from keeper covering: system make/model, recording method,
time-stamp accuracy method, retention, access. Master copy preserved.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Brien; DPP v O'Carroll.
✗ Recognition Without Foundation DPP v Cooney / Casey
THE ISSUE: 'I recognised the accused on CCTV' without articulating prior familiarity.
WHY IT MATTERS: Recognition admissibility requires foundation of familiarity established by
witness's own evidence.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence: 'On what basis do you recognise the accused?' Vague
answers undermine the identification.
HOW TO PREVENT: Statement structure: foundation first (length of acquaintance, nature of
contacts, identifying features), then recognition act, then basis (specific features visible on footage).
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Cooney; DPP v Casey; Turnbull warning.
✗ Single Photograph of Injury Best practice
THE ISSUE: Photograph of injury taken once on day of incident; no follow-up as bruising develops.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

WHY IT MATTERS: Bruising develops over 24-48 hours. Single photograph underestimates
harm.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Medical evidence may not capture full extent without
photographs.
HOW TO PREVENT: Photograph day-of AND 24-48 hours later AND 7 days later for serious
injury. Multiple angles. Scale ruler where possible.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.3 NFOAPA; s.4 NFOAPA; DV practice.
✗ Forensic Contamination at Scene DPP v JF
THE ISSUE: Inadequate PPE in inner cordon; cross-contamination between exhibits; same officer
handling suspect and victim items.
WHY IT MATTERS: DNA / fingerprint / fibre evidence depends on contamination-free chain.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence expert identifies contamination opportunities. Sample
integrity challenged.
HOW TO PREVENT: PPE before inner cordon. Single-use suits per scene. No
eating/drinking/phone. Separate officers for suspect and victim items where possible.
Decontamination between.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v JF; DPP v Bowes; CJ(FE&DNAS)A 2014.

10.6 Mistakes in Disclosure
✗ Withholding 'Unhelpful' Material DPP v Ward / Meleady
THE ISSUE: Material that seems to favour defence not disclosed.
WHY IT MATTERS: Defence assesses relevance, not prosecution. Withholding is breach.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Material discovered late or never; conviction at risk on appeal.
HOW TO PREVENT: Disclose everything relevant to guilt or innocence. Flag uncertain cases to
DPP.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Ward; DPP v Meleady; DPP v Wilson.
✗ Treating Disclosure as One-Off DPP v Wilson
THE ISSUE: Disclosure file closed at start of trial; new material not disclosed.
WHY IT MATTERS: Disclosure obligation continues through trial.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: New material emerges; conviction vitiated on appeal.
HOW TO PREVENT: Disclosure schedule maintained as living document through trial.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Wilson; DPP v Ward.
✗ Unilateral PII Withholding DPP v Marley
THE ISSUE: Sensitive material withheld without court PII application.
WHY IT MATTERS: PII is court-managed, not unilateral.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence discovers withholding; case may be stayed.
HOW TO PREVENT: Identify sensitive material early. Brief DPP. PII application via court
process.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Marley; DPP v Ward.
✗ Lost Notebook Undisclosed DPP v O'Keeffe
THE ISSUE: Garda notebook lost; not disclosed until trial.
WHY IT MATTERS: Notebooks are disclosable. Loss is serious matter requiring explanation.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Late disclosure of loss damages case credibility.
HOW TO PREVENT: Document loss promptly. Search efforts logged. Statement on the loss
provided.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v O'Keeffe; DPP v Tuite.

10.7 Mistakes in Witness Handling
✗ Cross-Contamination Between Witnesses (recurring)
THE ISSUE: Multiple witnesses to incident allowed to discuss before separate statements.
WHY IT MATTERS: Statements should be free of contamination by other accounts.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Statements line up too neatly; defence highlights coordination.
HOW TO PREVENT: Separate witnesses physically at scene. Statement-taking individually.
Instruct not to discuss.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Statement-taking discipline.
✗ Single Statement Assumption DPP v Yates
THE ISSUE: Single statement taken; witness later retracts or becomes hostile; no s.16 protection.
WHY IT MATTERS: Single statement leaves the case exposed to retraction.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Trial witness retracts; no admissible alternative.
HOW TO PREVENT: S.16 CJA 2006 sworn statement at first opportunity for any witness where
retraction risk (DV, gang-associated, vulnerable).
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Yates; s.16 CJA 2006.
✗ Witness Not Prepared for Court (recurring)
THE ISSUE: Witness arrives at court cold — no re-read of statement, no orientation.
WHY IT MATTERS: Cold witnesses give poor evidence under cross-examination.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Inconsistencies emerge under cross; confidence collapses.
HOW TO PREVENT: Pre-trial contact: re-read statement, explain procedure, confirm availability.
For vulnerable: pre-trial court visit.
CROSS-REFERENCES: Witness preparation.
✗ Treating Witness Intimidation as Talk s.41 CJA 1999
THE ISSUE: Witness reports approach / threat; not charged separately, not escalated.
WHY IT MATTERS: Intimidation is a serious offence and triggers protection considerations.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Witness withdraws; case collapses.
HOW TO PREVENT: Charge intimidation under s.41 CJA 1999. Special measures application.
Liaison with Witness Protection where appropriate.
CROSS-REFERENCES: s.41 CJA 1999; CEA 1992 (measures); witness protection.

10.8 Mistakes at Charge / File / Court
✗ Charge Wording Errors (recurring)
THE ISSUE: CM13 cites wrong section, wrong Act, wrong date.
WHY IT MATTERS: Errors propagate. Amendments possible but defence point.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence challenges charge precision.
HOW TO PREVENT: Read CM13 carefully before signing. Cross-check section, Act, date,
particulars.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Ó Súilleabháin; CM13 discipline.
✗ Overcharging / Undercharging DPP v Ó Súilleabháin
THE ISSUE: Charging s.3 NFOAPA when only s.2 evidence; charging robbery when force was not
part of theft.
WHY IT MATTERS: Charge selection should match evidence.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Charge may be dismissed or amended at trial; case weakened.
HOW TO PREVENT: Identify all available charges, assess evidence against elements, select what
matches.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Ó Súilleabháin; specific offence statutes.
✗ Late Additions to Book of Evidence CPA 1967 ss.4-K
THE ISSUE: New witness or exhibit appears post-service.
WHY IT MATTERS: Defence entitled to notice and time. Late additions disrupt trial.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence applies for adjournment; trial delayed.
HOW TO PREVENT: Anticipate during investigation. All material in initial Book where possible.
Where addition necessary, prompt notice.
CROSS-REFERENCES: CPA 1967; CJA 1999.
✗ Court Testimony Inconsistencies DPP v Tuite
THE ISSUE: Testimony differs from statement; speculation beyond evidence.
WHY IT MATTERS: Garda evidence anchored in statement. Inconsistencies are defence material.
HOW IT UNFOLDS IN COURT: Defence cross-references trial testimony against statement.
HOW TO PREVENT: Re-read statement and notebook before trial. Stay anchored. 'I don't recall'
is honest and acceptable. Don't speculate.
CROSS-REFERENCES: DPP v Tuite; statement preparation.

WORKING REFERENCE — NOT LEGAL ADVICE
Case law evolves. Always check the live statute and most recent appellate authority before relying in evidence
or in court. Citations are working references; verify exact neutral citations on Bailii / courts.ie before quoting
in any formal file.

How to Use This Reference
Each case is presented in four parts in the middle column: FACTS (what happened in the case), PRINCIPLE

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

(what the court held and why), RATIO (the binding principle distilled), and TEST (what to ask yourself when
applying it). The right-hand column translates each principle into operational practice — what you actually do
differently at scene, in custody, in interview, or in court because of that case.
The structure follows the operational chronology: arrest powers, search and entry, detention regimes, evidence
and admissibility, use of force, then specific offence categories (drugs, public order, theft and property,
domestic violence, road traffic, organised crime), then the cross-cutting topics (complex investigations, covert
policing, interview law, disclosure, constitutional rights, forensics), and finally inner-city Dublin operational
themes and case preparation for DPP directions.
After the case-law tables, you will find: nine Operational Workflows (arrest, drugs search execution, DV
callout, CCTV capture, inference warning sequence, scene preservation, building a s.15 case, stop and search
articulation, PEACE interview strategy); a Statutory Anchors index cross-referencing all statutes cited; standard
caution and warning wordings; and seven operational mantras to carry through every interaction.
Use the reference forwards (case-law → operational application) when learning or preparing, and backwards
(need a practical answer → find the case-law foundation) when handling a specific situation. Cross-references
between sections are deliberate — a single operational situation often engages multiple legal frameworks.

1. Arrest, Caution & Suspect Rights
The foundation. Every coercive act flows from a lawful arrest. Get the arrest wrong and the case is gone. Each
Garda must form their own reasonable suspicion at the moment of arrest, communicate the reason in language
understood, and apply the caution before any incriminating question.

v FACTS: Leachinsky arrested under a local

the citizen has a right to know why. Without

DPP v Shaw [1982] FACTS: Shaw held for 44 hours after arrest
for
kidnap/murder,
primarily
for

PRINCIPLE: Walsh J held that detention
purely for questioning, without specific
statutory authority, amounts to unlawful
imprisonment. Griffin J introduced

possible saving of evidence — but the
principle stands: no common law power to
detain for questioning. RATIO: Either
statutory power exists (s.4 CJA 1984, s.30
OASA 1939, s.50 CJA 2007, s.16 CJ(DT)A
1996) or the person must be released. The

Irish law. TEST: At every moment of
detention, can a specific statutory power be

Facts, Ratio & Reasoning
Multiple investigative interests do not
vitiate s.30; bad faith does. If the scheduled
offence belief is a sham — manufactured to
use the more powerful regime — the arrest
is unlawful. RATIO: The bona fides of the
scheduled offence suspicion is the linchpin.
TEST: Was the scheduled offence suspicion
genuine and based on grounds, OR was it
manufactured?
FACTS: Boylan detained under s.4 CJA
1984. Detention interrupted by solicitor
consultation and medical attention.
Question: do these periods count toward
6/12/24-hour limits? PRINCIPLE: The
CCA held that periods during which the
detained person is receiving medical
attention, consulting a solicitor, or in rest,
are excluded from the detention clock under
s.4(8) CJA 1984. RATIO: The detention
clock measures time available for
investigation, not time absent from
interview for proper purposes. Exclusions

4. Evidence & Admissibility
Voluntariness, continuity, disclosure — the three pillars of evidence surviving challenge. The Garda's task is
not just to gather evidence but to gather it lawfully, preserve it intact, and disclose everything relevant to guilt
or innocence. JC modified Kenny but did not license corner-cutting.

v

custody; challenged voluntariness on

PRINCIPLE: Walsh J set the voluntariness
test for confessions: was the confession
given freely, without threat, inducement, or
oppression? The will of the suspect must
not have been overborne. The test is
whether the prosecution can prove beyond
reasonable doubt that the confession was
voluntary. RATIO: Voluntariness is the
touchstone of confession admissibility.
Inducement need not be overt — any
promise of advantage or threat of
disadvantage from a person in authority

Practical Application
Things that taint voluntariness: 'It'll go
easier if you tell us', 'Your girlfriend is
in the next room — we can arrest her
too', 'We have your prints' (when you
don't — false statements are not strictly
inducement but undermine fairness),
'You can go home as soon as you tell
us', 'The judge will look kindly on
cooperation', 'We'll speak to the DPP
for you'. Avoid ALL of these. Stay
factual. Present evidence; ask account;
allow response. Don't trade. Don't
bargain. Don't threaten. Don't befriend
manipulatively. The confession that
survives is the one freely given.

S.18 Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997 is the statutory anchor for police use of force. Subjective
belief in necessity, objective reasonableness in the circumstances as the Garda believed them. Document the
force continuum: presence, verbal, contact, less-lethal, lethal — and why you climbed each rung.

S.18
Non-Fatal STATUTORY FRAMEWORK: Codifies
Offences Against lawful use of force. Force is lawful where
the Person Act 1997 the person believes it necessary for: (a) selfdefence or defence of others, (b) defence of
property, (c) prevention of crime, (d) lawful
arrest, (e) prevention of escape from lawful
detention. CONDITIONS: (1) Honest belief
in necessity (subjective), (2) Force
reasonable in the circumstances as the
person believed them to be (combined
subjective/objective). RATIO: The test
combines subjective honest belief with
objective reasonableness assessed against
the believed circumstances. A mistaken
belief held honestly does not defeat the
defence unless the mistake was
unreasonable. TEST: (1) Did you believe
force was necessary? (2) Was the level of
force reasonable given what you believed?

(1) Did the accused honestly believe force

excessive? (3) If yes — verdict is
manslaughter, not murder (or full acquittal
if force believed reasonable).

TEST: (1) Was the arrest lawful? (2) Was

force proportionate?

8. Theft, Burglary, Robbery
The Criminal Justice (Theft & Fraud Offences) Act 2001 codified most of this regime. Theft requires dishonest
appropriation with intent to deprive. Burglary requires entry as trespasser with intent to commit arrestable
offence — or commission after entry. Aggravated burglary adds weapon; robbery adds force or threat at the
time of theft.

(CCA)
on basis it was inadmissible opinion.
PRINCIPLE: The CCA distinguished
identification
from
recognition.
Identification = identifying a stranger from
observation. Recognition = identifying
someone the witness already knows.
Recognition evidence by Garda admissible
where familiarity is established by the
witness's
own
evidence.
RATIO:
Recognition is more reliable than
identification because the brain processes
known faces differently. TEST: (1) Does
the witness have prior familiarity? (2) Is the
basis articulated? (3) Are conditions of
viewing reasonable?

inconsistent

prior
statement;

evidence at trial materially inconsistent

calling party. Prior statement may be
admitted as evidence of truth under s.16
CJA 2006 where conditions met
(voluntariness, reliability, fair trial).
RATIO: Witness retraction does not destroy
prior evidence — system allows the prior
statement to be evidence in its own right.
TEST: (1) Is the witness genuinely hostile
(not merely unhelpful)? (2) Is the prior
statement reliable and voluntary? (3) Is
admission in interests of justice?

13. Informants, Surveillance & Covert Policing
Source protection is presumptive; innocence-at-stake is the narrow exception. CHIS handling separate from
main investigation file. Surveillance Act 2009 authorisation regime strict — short-term Superintendent, longerterm District Judge. Intercept under 1993 Act requires Ministerial authorisation and reserved for most serious
offences.

14. Interview Law & Strategy
Caution before any incriminating question. Solicitor consultation before inference warnings under
ss.18/19/19A CJA 1984. PEACE model (Planning, Engage & Explain, Account, Closure, Evaluation) produces
better evidence than confession-seeking. Strict statutory compliance for inferences — read warnings verbatim
from prescribed form.

15. Disclosure & Fair Trial
Disclose everything relevant to guilt or innocence — and document what you sought even if you didn't find it.
The duty is active (Braddish) and continuing (Wilson). PII for sensitive material managed by court via DPP
application, never unilateral withholding. JC is a safety net for inadvertent breaches, not a strategy.

Trial preparation: ensure every relevant
matter is on the record. If there is a
procedural defect, an admissibility
argument, a disclosure issue — get it
before the trial judge. Don't 'reserve' it
for appeal. Build the record.

Trial-period discipline: any new
development — witness contact,
additional CCTV located, intelligence
received, change in evidence —
immediately notify prosecutor. Written
record on file. Don't filter — let
prosecutor and DPP assess. Late
disclosure is a real risk to convictions
on appeal.

Notebook discipline: contemporaneous
entries, preserved per force policy
(typically multi-year retention for
serious matters). Don't lose them.
Where lost: prompt notification, search
efforts documented. Statement of
evidence may draw on the notebook
AND be independently sufficient — but
lost notebook is a defence point.

Practical: every coercive act is
assessable against ECHR. Article 5 —
liberty (arrest grounds, detention
conditions, judicial oversight). Article 6
— fair trial (legal representation,
silence, evidence). Article 8 — private
life (search, surveillance, data).
Document proportionality reasoning.

16. Constitutional & Human Rights
Articles 38.1 (fair trial), 40.3 (personal rights), 40.4 (liberty), 40.5 (dwelling) + ECHR Articles 5, 6, 8.
Proportionality runs through every coercive power. Damache requires judicial issuer for dwelling warrants.
Heaney articulates the proportionality test for restrictions on rights.

17. Scene Preservation & Forensics
Chain of custody starts at the scene. Contamination = case lost. First responder priorities: preserve life,
preserve scene, cordon, log, call. PPE before entry to inner cordon. Each exhibit individually sealed and
labelled. CJ(FE&DNAS)A 2014 governs DNA samples and database. Phones: power off, Faraday bag, CCU
— do not browse.

Operational Workflows — Law into Practice
These workflows translate the case-law principles into step-by-step station and scene practice. Use them as
checklists when planning operations and as audit tools when reviewing actions. Each step reflects a specific
legal requirement — skip a step and a case-law authority may come back to undermine the case.

A. Arrest Workflow (s.4 CJA 1984 — standard arrestable offence)
• Pre-arrest: form your OWN reasonable suspicion (Walsh). Document the grounds in your notebook
BEFORE the act.
• On arrest: state the offence + section + Act (Christie v Leachinsky). 'You are under arrest for
[offence] contrary to [section] of [Act].'
• Caution: 'You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so, but anything you say will
be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.' (Pringle, Conroy)
• Note time, place, and persons present at arrest. Witness Garda where possible.
• Search incident to arrest (s.6 CJA 1984) — person and immediate effects (Fagan).
• At station: deliver to Member-in-Charge. Custody record opens. Clock starts (s.4).
• Member-in-Charge informs of rights: solicitor, family notification, medical, vulnerable adult
assessment.
• Solicitor consulted before any interview (Gormley & White, Doyle).
• Caution administered again before EACH interview session and after EACH break.
• Reviews: 6 hours (Superintendent for further +6), 12 hours total (Chief Superintendent for further
+12), maximum 24 hours absent re-arrest grounds (O'Brien).
• Custody Regulations 1987 adhered to throughout — meals, sleep, breaks (Buck, Bowes &
McGrath).
• On release/charge: timed entries, property returned (or detained with explanation), bail conditions
explained.

B. Drugs Search Warrant Execution (s.26 MDA / s.7 CJA 2006)
• Pre-execution briefing: read warrant — address, offence, date, signature, named member or 'any
member'. Verify accuracy. (Dunne)
•

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

children/vulnerable, dynamic vs slow entry.
• Approach: knock, identify ('Gardaí — search warrant'), state authority, present warrant copy on
demand (Lavery).
• Force entry only on refusal, evidence destruction risk, or where warrant expressly authorises forced
entry.
• On entry: note time of entry, all occupants present, immediate observations.
• Systematic search by room — log each searcher's area. Photograph in situ before seizing.
• Each exhibit: number, location, time, finder, photograph, seal in tamper-evident bag, signature
across seal.
• Field test (if equipped) for indicative result — FSI confirmatory test required for evidence.
Photograph the test reagent reaction.
• Cash: count with witness, denomination breakdown, photograph in situ.
• Phones: power off if possible, Faraday bag, CCU for extraction (Forde, Egan). Do NOT browse
content.
• Each occupant: search under s.23 MDA, statement at scene (verbatim, including denials),
continuity from search to station.
• Items in plain view of unrelated offences (e.g. firearms during drugs search) — seize under s.7
CJA 2006 (Heaphy). Photograph in situ. Consider further warrant if continuing search.
• Time of departure logged. Premises secured (or sealed if vacated for further forensic work).
• PULSE incident raised. Continuity statements from each Garda involved. Property log entries.

C. Domestic Violence Callout
• Risk-assess approach: known history (PULSE check en route), reports of weapons, vulnerable
persons.
• Two-Garda response standard. Body-worn camera deployed (where issued).
• On scene: separate parties physically into different rooms with one Garda each.
• Check welfare of children present — Tusla notification considered (Children First Act 2015).
• Photograph injuries immediately — multiple angles, with scale. Re-photograph 24-48 hours later
as bruising develops.
• Statement from injured party — aim for s.16 CJA 2006 sworn statement format (peace
commissioner if available).
• Scene photographs: damaged items, weapons, environment. Note positions before disturbing.
• Check PULSE for prior incidents, live DVA orders. Breach of order = arrest under s.30 DVA 2018.
• Positive arrest where evidence supports — don't mediate (DVA philosophy). Charge selection:
assault (s.2/3/4 NFOAPA), threats (s.5 NFOAPA), criminal damage, breach of order, coercive
control (s.39 DVA 2018) where pattern available.
• Coercive control: schedule full follow-up statement covering pattern of behaviour, timeline,
controlling conduct, isolation, financial control, monitoring.
• Medical attention as required — document attendance, refusal, or recommendation.
• Safety planning with victim: refuge contacts, support services, Garda Victim Liaison.
• Application for protection/safety/barring/emergency barring order — facilitate access to court.
• Follow-up contact within 7 days — additional statement, photograph development of injuries,
check on welfare.

D. CCTV Evidence Capture
• Identify potential sources within 1km radius of scene: shops, pubs, transport, council cameras,
private homes with doorbell cameras, taxis.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Approach within 24-72 hours — many systems overwrite within 7-30 days (Braddish, Dunne).
• Written preservation request to keeper: date/time range, indication of what's sought, who can
authorise release.
• Statement from keeper covering: system make/model, recording method (DVR/NVR/cloud), timestamp accuracy (NTP sync / manual / drift), retention period, who has access.
• Master copy preserved on original media or sealed forensic image. Working copy for case file
(DVD/USB/digital).
• Exhibit label: source, date downloaded, time range, format, downloaded by, signature.
• Authentication statement from downloader: process used, no manipulation, full sequence retained.
• Where Garda recognition: separate statement on basis of familiarity (Cooney, Casey) BEFORE the
identification act.
• Show context to court — full sequence including period before/after key moment, not just
prejudicial segment.
• Disclosure to defence: master copy or certified working copy with format playable on standard
equipment.

E. Inference Warning Procedure (ss.18 / 19 / 19A CJA 1984)
• Pre-condition: solicitor consulted (Connolly). Verify on record — 'You have consulted your
solicitor [name] at [time]. Is that correct?'
• Read the prescribed warning VERBATIM from the station-issued form. Do NOT paraphrase. The
form has different wording for ss.18, 19, and 19A — use the correct one.
• Pause. Ask 'Do you understand what I have just said to you?' Note response.
• Offer further solicitor consultation: 'Do you wish to consult your solicitor again before I ask you a
specific question?'
• Pause for any consultation taken. Resume on record.
• Specific question:
• s.18 — 'How do you account for [specific item/substance/mark] found [specific location] [specific
time]?'
• s.19 — 'You were seen at [place] at [time]. How do you account for your presence at that place at
that time?'
• s.19A — Explain the provision and invite the suspect to give an account of any defence they may
later rely on.
• Note response verbatim. Silence is silence — do not characterise.
• Re-caution before continuing standard interview.
• Document the entire sequence with timestamps. Recording continues throughout.

F. Scene Preservation — Serious Crime
• First Garda priorities: (1) preserve life — first aid, ambulance called, (2) preserve scene — do NOT
move body or objects unless life-saving, (3) cordon, (4) log, (5) call.
• Establish OUTER cordon broad (suspect escape route, witnesses, secondary scenes, vehicles) and
INNER cordon close (immediate scene, weapon, key evidence).
• Common Approach Path (CAP) — single route in/out for everyone. Cap entrances marked with
tape.
• Scene log: every person entering inner cordon — time in, time out, purpose, by whose
authorisation.
• Restrict access — even other Gardaí. PPE before inner cordon (suit, gloves, mask, overshoes).
Single-use per scene.
• Notify: Sergeant → Inspector → SIO. Scenes of Crime. FSI as required. Coroner if death.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Photography Section.
• Photograph as found before any movement — overall, mid-range, close-up. Sketch with
measurements.
• Witnesses separated and statements taken while memory fresh. Don't let them confer.
• Note environment: weather, temperature, lighting, ambient noise, smells. Time of sunrise/sunset
relevant.
• Initial briefing for SIO on arrival. Decision log opened.
• Press Office only for media (DPP v Independent News & Media).

G. Building a s.15 MDA Sale/Supply Case
• Quantity beyond personal use — articulate threshold by reference to typical user consumption
(Foley, Power).
• Packaging: deal bags in standard amounts (€20, €50, €100 equivalents). Photograph quantities and
arrangement.
• Paraphernalia: scales (digital or balance), plastic bags, cling film, mixing/cutting agents, spoons,
weighing surfaces.
• Cash: denomination breakdown — €20s and €50s in quantity consistent with dealing. Count with
witness, photograph denominations.
• Mobile phones: dealer line separate from personal often. Messages re supply ('any sound for
tonight', 'how much', 'on the way'). CCU for proper extraction.
• Notebook/ledger of debts — 'tick book'. Names, amounts, dates.
• Surveillance/observation of handovers if pre-arrest — statement from observing member with
times, distances, descriptions, visibility conditions.
• Lifestyle evidence: declared income (Revenue check), benefits claimed, expenditure observed
(vehicles, clothing, jewellery, holidays), frequent visitors at known short intervals (Casey
recognition possible).
• Joint possession analysis where multiple occupants — Reilly factors (knowledge + control for
each).
• FSI valuation report — for s.15A threshold (€13,000 market value at offence date) (Power).
• Money laundering consideration where cash significant — Proceeds of Crime / CAB referral.
• Building the case for DPP: file should establish dealing pattern beyond reasonable doubt with each
strand independently sound.

H. Stop & Search Articulation (s.23 MDA / s.30 OASA / s.30 CJ(PO)A 1994)
• Articulate reasonable suspicion BEFORE the stop — document in notebook with timestamp.
• Specific facts: 'I observed [conduct] at [place] at [time] in circumstances indicating [type of
offence] including [specific factors].'
• Specific is good — 'observed handover behaviour consistent with dealing in known dealing area
at time consistent with dealing pattern'.
• Vague is bad — 'looked suspicious', 'acting nervous', 'known to police'.
• Identify yourself: in uniform = identification by uniform; plain clothes = present warrant card.
• State the power: 'I am stopping you under section 23 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977' or applicable.
• State the grounds in brief: 'I have reasonable grounds to suspect you are in possession of controlled
drugs because [specific reason].'
• Witness Garda present where possible.
• Search proportionate to suspicion: outer clothing, pockets, bag. Intimate searches only on specific
power and procedure.
• Document items found, location on person, time. Photograph if significant.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Statement at scene from person — verbatim response. Note any admissions or denials.
• Stop-and-search form (where applicable) — complete fully on the day. PULSE entry.
• If nothing found: explain ('I have completed my search and am satisfied no offence appears to have
been committed. Thank you for your cooperation.'). Release courteously. Even-handed practice is
critical.

I. PEACE Interview Strategy
• PLAN & PREPARE: read all evidence, identify topics in sequence, plan question structure,
anticipate accounts, identify evidence to deploy and when.
• Written interview plan before entering room — topics, key questions, evidence cues. Update plan
between sessions.
• ENGAGE & EXPLAIN: introductions on record (your name, rank, station, witness Garda), caution
administered, purpose of interview stated, ground rules (breaks available, solicitor accessible, no
obligation to answer).
• ACCOUNT: get the account first. Open question — 'Tell me about your day on [date]' — and let
the suspect speak. Active listening, minimal interruption.
• Probe stage: open questions before closed. 'Tell me more about…' before 'Did you…?' Cover gaps
in the account.
• Challenge stage: with evidence, not assertion. 'CCTV shows you at [place] at [time]. Can you
explain that?' Not 'we know you did it'.
• Avoid: leading questions on key facts; multi-part questions (gives suspect choice); repeated
questions on same point (oppression — Holland, Ward); promises or inducements (Tyndall).
• Inference warnings deployed where appropriate per Procedure E.
• CLOSURE: summarise the suspect's account on record, give opportunity to add or correct, end
formally on tape.
• EVALUATION: review interview after, identify gaps, plan further session if appropriate. Note
evidential value of admissions, denials, lies (lies are evidence too).

Standard Cautions, Warnings & Wordings
Standard Arrest Caution
"You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so, but anything you say will be taken
down in writing and may be given in evidence."
Administered: at arrest, on entry to interview room, after every break, before resumed questioning.
Do not paraphrase.

Inference Warning Sequence (ss.18/19/19A CJA 1984)
1. Confirm solicitor consultation on record with time.
2. Read prescribed warning verbatim from station-issued form (different wording for each section).
3. Pause. Ask 'Do you understand?' Note response.
4. Offer further solicitor consultation. Allow if requested.
5. Ask the specific statutory question (s.18 item/mark; s.19 presence; s.19A defence fact).
6. Note response verbatim. Silence is silence.
7. Re-caution before continuing standard interview.

RTA Specimen Demand (s.12 RTA 2010)
"I require you under section 12 of the Road Traffic Act 2010 to provide [breath / blood / urine]
specimen. Failure or refusal to comply without reasonable excuse is an offence and may result in
disqualification from driving and prosecution."

Detention Review Schedule (s.4 CJA 1984)
• Hour 0-6: Initial detention period authorised by Member-in-Charge on grounds at custody.
• Hour 6-12: Extension by Superintendent's written direction. Grounds documented.
• Hour 12-24: Further extension by Chief Superintendent's written direction. Investigative necessity
articulated (Finn).
• Periodic welfare reviews per Custody Regulations.
• Clock suspensions logged: medical attention, solicitor consultation, rest period (Boylan).

Operational Mantras
Seven principles distilled from the case-law. Internalise these and the legal architecture supports rather than
constrains policing.
Name the power.
If you can't name the section, don't do the act. Every coercive action has a statutory or common-law
authority — articulate it before you act, document it as you act, and defend it after you act.
Document contemporaneously.
Your notebook on the day is worth more than your memory in court. Statements drawn from
notebooks; notebooks drawn from contemporaneous observation. Backfilled records are visible —

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

and devastating — in cross-examination.
Proportionate, not maximum.
Use the least intrusive power that achieves the policing purpose. Heaney proportionality runs
through every coercive decision. Where you used force, search, or detention beyond what was
necessary, the case-law will find you.
Disclose everything.
Withholding what you should disclose ends careers and cases. The duty is active (Braddish),
continuing (Wilson), and broader than 'helpful to prosecution'. Defence relevance, not prosecution
preference, is the test.
Continuity is sacred.
Seal it, sign it, log it, lock it. From scene to lab to court, the chain of custody is the case.
Contamination, gaps, unexplained handling — each ends the evidential value of an exhibit and may
end the case.
Voluntariness wins cases.
No promises, no threats, no tricks. Every interview is reviewable on tape. Subtle inducement
(Tyndall) is as fatal as oppression (Holland, Ward). The Garda's task is to elicit truth, not engineer
confession.
Article 40.5.
The dwelling is inviolable. The threshold is the highest in policing. Warrant, consent, statutory
power, hot pursuit, or genuine emergency — and document which one you relied on, every time.
End of Reference
Working reference document — verify exact neutral citations on Bailii and courts.ie before any formal use.
Case law evolves; check the most recent appellate authority on any point before relying.
