Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1. The Architecture of Proof
1.1 Burden and standard of proof
The prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt. This is the “golden thread” running through the criminal
law — Woolmington v DPP [1935] AC 462 — carried into Irish law and grounded in the right to trial in due course
of law under Article 38.1 of the Constitution. “Beyond reasonable doubt” is a high standard: not proof beyond all
doubt, but proof to the point where the tribunal is sure. The accused is presumed innocent; if at the end of the case
the tribunal is left with a reasonable doubt, the accused is acquitted.
Two distinct burdens must be kept apart:

• Legal (persuasive) burden — the obligation to prove a fact to the required standard. On the ingredients of
the offence this rests on the prosecution from start to finish and never shifts. It is discharged only to the
criminal standard.
• Evidential burden — the lighter obligation to put enough material before the court to make an issue live. A
defence such as self-defence, provocation, duress or automatism carries an evidential burden on the accused:
there must be some evidence fit to be left to the jury. Once it is raised, the legal burden of disproving it (to
the criminal standard) generally returns to the prosecution.
Reverse onus and statutory presumptions. Some statutes place a legal burden on the accused, usually to the lower
civil standard (balance of probabilities). Operationally important examples include the presumption of intent to
supply once a defined quantity is proved under s.15 of the Misuse of Drugs Act 1977, and possession presumptions
in the firearms code. Two points matter for the investigator: (a) the prosecution must still prove the primary fact (the
quantity, the possession) beyond reasonable doubt before the presumption is engaged, and (b) the presumption is
rebuttable. Build the case on the primary fact; do not assume the presumption does the work for you.
Operational consequence
Build the file as if the defence will say nothing. Every ingredient must stand on prosecution evidence alone.
An investigation that depends on the suspect failing to explain has been built backwards — silence is the
suspect’s right, and the inference provisions (§5) are narrow and heavily safeguarded.

1.2 Facts in issue, relevance, admissibility and weight
There are four gates. First, identify the facts in issue — the ingredients the prosecution must prove and any defence
raised. Evidence must then be relevant (it makes a fact in issue more or less probable). It must be admissible (not
shut out by a rule of law such as hearsay, or by the trial judge’s discretion to exclude where prejudice outweighs
probative value). Only then does the tribunal of fact assess weight. The investigator’s job is to make sure admissible
evidence is not lost through avoidable error — most evidence is lost at the admissibility gate (a defective warrant, a
broken chain of custody, an uncautioned admission), not on weight.

1.3 Functions of judge and jury; the voir dire
• Questions of law — admissibility, sufficiency (whether there is a case to answer), and the legal directions —
are for the judge.
• Questions of fact — whose account to accept, what weight to give, ultimately guilt — are for the jury (or the
judge sitting alone in the District Court and the Special Criminal Court).
The voir dire (“trial within a trial”). Where the admissibility of disputed evidence turns on contested facts — most
commonly the voluntariness of a confession or the lawfulness of a search or detention — the judge hears the issue
in the absence of the jury, makes findings of fact, and rules. The arresting member, the member in charge and the
interviewers are the witnesses on the voir dire. The custody record, the notebook entries and the electronic interview
recording are the documents that decide it. Write every entry as though it will be read aloud, line by line, on a voir
dire — because in a contested case it will be.

2.1 Circumstantial evidence — the strands in a cord
Circumstantial evidence is not “weaker” than direct evidence; many of the strongest convictions rest on it. The settled
approach treats individual items as strands in a cord: no single strand may bear the weight, but woven together they
may exclude any reasonable hypothesis consistent with innocence. Where a case is wholly circumstantial, the
tribunal must be satisfied that guilt is the only reasonable inference open on the evidence as a whole. The
investigator’s task is to gather and preserve every strand — CCTV across multiple premises, ANPR, cell-site and
handset data, forensic transfer, recent possession of property, lies told in interview, opportunity and motive — and
to record exactly how each was obtained so the chain survives challenge.
Inner-city application
A typical Mountjoy contested case (a street robbery, a non-dwelling burglary, a supply detection) is built
circumstantially: CCTV stitched across several premises, recovery of property or proceeds, forensic links, and
movement data. Map the strands early and identify which one the defence will pull — usually identification
or continuity — and reinforce it before charge, not after.

3. Hearsay
The rule. An out-of-court statement — oral, written, or by conduct — is inadmissible when adduced to prove the
truth of its contents. The rationale: the maker is not on oath, cannot be cross-examined, and the tribunal cannot assess
their demeanour, so the reliability of the assertion cannot be tested. The rule is wide. It catches the obvious (“she
told me he did it”) and the less obvious (a witness reading another person’s note into evidence, or relaying what a
colleague observed).

3.1 What is not hearsay
• Statements adduced to prove they were made, not that they were true — to show notice, a demand, a threat
actually uttered, or the state of mind of the person who heard it.
• Original (operative) words that have legal effect in themselves — the words that constitute the offence: the
threat in a s.5 NFOAP threat to kill, the demand in a robbery, the menacing or grossly offensive message
itself, the words of an agreement.
• Real evidence of a machine’s automatic output (an ATM transaction log, an intoximeter print-out) where no
human assertion is involved — though provenance and authentication are still required.
• Implied assertions and prior statements of the witness themselves are treated with care — a witness’s own
earlier out-of-court statement, repeated by another, is hearsay subject to the exceptions below.

3.2 The principal exceptions

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Admissions and confessions. A statement against interest by the accused is admissible against them, subject to
voluntariness and the exclusionary rule (see §4 and §6). This is the single most litigated hearsay exception in practice.
Res gestae. Spontaneous statements so closely bound to the event that the risk of concoction is minimal — the
distressed 999 call, the immediate excited utterance at the scene, words accompanying and explaining an act.
Persuasive guidance: Ratten v R [1972] AC 378; R v Andrews [1987] AC 281.
Dying declarations. A statement by a person who believes their death is imminent, admissible on a charge
concerning their own homicide. Narrow and rare, but live in fatal assaults where the victim speaks before death.
Statements in furtherance of a common design. The acts and declarations of one participant in a joint enterprise,
made in furtherance of it, may be admissible against the others.
Documentary / business records. Records compiled in the ordinary course of a business are admissible as evidence
of the facts in them under ss.5–6 Criminal Evidence Act 1992, on proof of the statutory conditions and usually by
certificate (see §11).
Statement of a witness who resiles. Under s.16 Criminal Justice Act 2006, a prior statement may, with leave, be
admitted as evidence of the facts in it where the witness — though available for cross-examination — refuses to
testify, denies making it, or gives inconsistent evidence, subject to reliability and fairness safeguards (see §13).
Why this matters at investigation stage
Capture res-gestae material at source: preserve the 999 audio, record the very first account verbatim in the
notebook with the time, and note the complainant’s demeanour and physical state. These materials are what
later allow the prosecution to rely on the exception when a witness goes cold or cannot attend.

4. Confessions and Admissions
4.1 The voluntariness rule
A confession is admissible only if made voluntarily. It is excluded if obtained by oppression, or by a threat or
inducement held out by a person in authority, or in circumstances that sap the free exercise of the suspect’s will.
Voluntariness is decided by the judge on the voir dire and the prosecution bears the onus of proving it. See People
(AG) v Cummins [1972] IR 312 and People (DPP) v Shaw [1982] IR 1. Separate from voluntariness, the court retains
a discretion to exclude a voluntary statement where, on the whole circumstances, admitting it would be unfair to the
accused.

• Oppression — conduct that saps the will: prolonged or hostile questioning, deprivation of rest, food or
medical attention, intimidation.
• Inducement — a threat or promise by a person in authority (“it’ll go easier if you talk”, “you can go home
once you sign”). Even a well-intentioned hint can vitiate voluntariness.
• Capacity — intoxication, withdrawal, youth, or mental disorder bear on whether the statement was truly the
product of a free choice.

4.2 The Judges’ Rules — in full
Origin and status. The Judges’ Rules were formulated by the judges of the King’s Bench — the first four in 1912,
a further five in 1918 — as guidance to police on questioning and the taking of statements. An Garda Síochána
continues to apply these original nine rules (Ireland did not adopt the 1964 English reformulation). They are rules of
practice, not rules of law: a breach does not make a statement automatically inadmissible, but it is a weighty factor,
and a deliberate or significant breach will ordinarily lead the court to exclude the statement in the exercise of its
discretion. They sit alongside, and are reinforced by, the constitutional protections, the Custody Regulations 1987
and the electronic-recording regime.
Rule I — Questioning to discover an offence
When a police officer is trying to discover whether, or by whom, an offence has been committed, he is entitled

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

to question any person, whether suspected or not, from whom he thinks that useful information may be
obtained.

Rule II — Caution once there are grounds to suspect
Whenever a police officer has made up his mind to charge a person with a crime, he should first caution such
person before asking any questions, or any further questions as the case may be.
Rule III — Persons in custody must be cautioned before questioning
Persons in custody should not be questioned without the usual caution being first administered.
Rule IV — Volunteered statements
If the prisoner wishes to volunteer any statement, the usual caution should be administered. It is desirable
that the last two words of such caution should be omitted, and that the caution should end with the words “be
given in evidence.”
Rule V — Form of the caution on charge
The caution to be administered to a prisoner when he is formally charged should be in the following words:
“Do you wish to say anything in answer to the charge? You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish
to do so, but whatever you say will be taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.” Care should be
taken to avoid any suggestion that his answers can only be used in evidence against him, as this may prevent
an innocent person making a statement which might help to clear him of the charge.
Rule VI — Statements made before there is time to caution
A statement made by a prisoner before there is time to caution him is not rendered inadmissible merely
because no caution has been given, but in such a case he should be cautioned as soon as possible.
Rule VII — No cross-examination of a volunteered statement
A prisoner making a voluntary statement must not be cross-examined, and no questions should be put to him
about it except for the purpose of removing ambiguity in what he has actually said — for example, where he
has mentioned an hour without saying whether morning or evening, or given a day of the week and a day of
the month that do not agree, he may be questioned only sufficiently to clear up the point.
Rule VIII — Co-accused statements
When two or more persons are charged with the same offence and statements are taken separately, the police
should not read these statements to the other persons charged. Each such person should be given a copy of
the statement, and nothing should be said or done to invite a reply. If a person charged then wishes to make
a statement in reply, the usual caution should first be administered.
Rule IX — Reduce statements to writing and have them adopted
Any statement made in accordance with the above rules should, whenever possible, be taken down in writing
and signed by the person making it, after it has been read over to him and he has been invited to make any
corrections he may wish.

How the Rules translate into the file
The custody record and interview recording must show: the caution administered in the correct form and
understood; questioning ceasing on charge; a volunteered statement preceded by caution and not crossexamined; co-accused statements handled under Rule VIII; and every statement read back, adopted and signed
under Rule IX. A clean record on these points defeats most Judges’-Rules challenges before they are made.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

4.3 Detention, custody regulations and access to a solicitor
• Detention under s.4 Criminal Justice Act 1984 must be lawful, properly authorised and extended, the custody
record complete, and the Custody Regulations 1987 observed — notification of rights, the role of the member
in charge, rest periods, food, and medical attention.
People (DPP) v Healy [1990] 2 IR 73
Holding / principle. Conscious denial or frustration of reasonable access to a solicitor renders the detention unlawful
and resulting admissions liable to exclusion. The right of reasonable access is a constitutional right.
People (DPP) v Gormley and White [2014] IESC 17
Holding / principle. A suspect who has requested legal advice must not be questioned until that advice has been
obtained; evidence (including forensic samples in White) taken in breach is liable to exclusion. The right to legal
advice attaches before and around questioning, not merely on paper.
People (DPP) v Doyle [2017] IESC 1
Holding / principle. Considered whether there is a right to have a solicitor physically present throughout interview.
The position is nuanced; current Garda practice permits solicitor presence on request. Record precisely what access
and presence were offered and provided. Verify the current operational instruction before interview.
Corroboration warning for confessions. Where a conviction would rest wholly or substantially on a confession,
s.10 of the Criminal Procedure Act 1993 requires the trial judge to consider warning the jury of the danger of
convicting on the confession alone. Always seek independent confirmation of facts admitted (the “special
knowledge” the suspect could only have if guilty, recovered property, forensic links).
Bullet-proofing the interview
Electronically record under the interview-recording regulations. Log solicitor access on the custody record
with exact times. Caution, and re-caution on every resumption. Remove ambiguity only — never crossexamine a volunteered statement (Rule VII). Read the memo back and have it adopted and signed (Rule IX).
A complete recording plus a clean custody record is the strongest defence to a voluntariness challenge.

5. Inferences from Silence
There is a constitutional right to silence. Statute permits a court, in defined circumstances and subject to strict
safeguards, to draw such inferences as appear proper from a suspect’s failure to mention or account for certain
matters. The principal provisions, as substituted by the Criminal Justice Act 2007, are:

• s.18 CJA 1984 — failure to account for objects, marks or substances on the person, clothing or footwear, or
in possession or in the place of arrest.
• s.19 CJA 1984 — failure to account for the suspect’s presence at a particular place at or about the time of the
offence.
• s.19A CJA 1984 — failure to mention, when questioned, a fact later relied on in defence that the suspect
could reasonably have been expected to mention.
Mandatory safeguards. An inference is permissible only where: the suspect was told in ordinary, non-technical
language what the failure might lead to; was given a reasonable opportunity to consult a solicitor before the relevant
failure; and the questioning was electronically recorded (or the suspect consented in writing to it not being recorded).
A failure on any safeguard defeats the inference. The regime’s constitutionality was upheld in Rock v Ireland [1997]
3 IR 484. Critically, an inference can corroborate but can never be the sole or main basis for a conviction.
Operational note
The inference provisions are tools to be deployed deliberately, with the correct statutory “request and
explanation” on the recording, solicitor access secured first, and the precise objects/marks/place identified. A
sloppy or generic invocation produces nothing usable. Treat them as a supplement to a case already built,
never as a substitute for evidence.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

6. The Exclusionary Rule
This governs evidence obtained in breach of a constitutional right (an unlawful search of a dwelling, an unlawful
detention) or otherwise illegally. It is the defence’s primary route to strike out the product of an investigation, so the
legal basis of every search, arrest and detention must be sound and recorded.
People (DPP) v JC [2015] IESC 31
Holding / principle. The modern test for unconstitutionally obtained evidence. Automatic exclusion (the old Kenny
rule) was replaced. Step one: was there a breach of a constitutional right? Step two: the prosecution may seek to
justify admission — where the breach was inadvertent or the product of a genuine, non-culpable error, the court has
a discretion to admit; where the breach was deliberate and conscious, reckless, or grossly negligent, the evidence is
presumptively excluded. The prosecution bears the onus of establishing the basis for admission.
Operational point. The defect that decides the case is the one created during the investigation. Get warrants right,
record the legal basis and the grounds for every search and arrest contemporaneously, and stay within the scope of
the power. A well-documented inadvertent error is survivable; a careless or unexplained breach is not.
People (DPP) v Kenny [1990] 2 IR 110
Holding / principle. The former position: evidence obtained in conscious and deliberate violation of constitutional
rights was excluded absolutely, save extraordinary excusing circumstances. Superseded by JC, but essential
background to the modern test and still cited.
Damache v DPP [2012] IESC 11
Holding / principle. A search warrant issued by a Garda who was part of the investigating team lacked the necessary
independence; the search of the dwelling was therefore unconstitutional. Warrants affecting the dwelling must issue
from an independent authority.
Operational point. For any dwelling search, confirm the warrant route is independent and the statutory grounds are
made out. The home attracts the highest constitutional protection under Article 40.5; errors here are rarely forgiven.
People (AG) v O'Brien [1965] IR 142
Holding / principle. Evidence obtained illegally but not in breach of a constitutional right is admitted or excluded
on a discretionary balancing of the public interest in prosecuting crime against the public interest in lawful State
conduct.

7. Identification Evidence
Honest witnesses can be, and are, mistaken — confidently. Identification is the most frequently fatal evidential
weakness in contested cases, and the area most within the investigator’s control. Treat every identification as fragile
until corroborated.
People (AG) v Casey (No. 2) [1963] IR 33
Holding / principle. Established the Casey warning: where a conviction depends wholly or substantially on visual
identification, the jury must be warned of the danger of acting on it, and of the possibility that an honest and
convincing witness may nonetheless be mistaken.
People (DPP) v Boyce [2008] IESC 62 (adopting R v Turnbull [1977] QB 224)
Holding / principle. The court must direct the jury to examine the circumstances of the identification using the
Turnbull factors, captured operationally by ADVOKATE: Amount of time under observation, Distance, Visibility,
Obstruction, Known to the witness, Any reason to remember, Time lapse since the event, Errors or discrepancies
between accounts.
Operational point. Take the ADVOKATE detail in the witness statement at the time, in the witness’s own words
— not reconstructed later. Where image quality is poor or identification is the sole proof, build corroboration:
recovered clothing, handset/IMEI, movement data, admissions on incidental facts.

7.1 Procedures and pitfalls

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Use a formal identification parade or a VIPER (video) procedure wherever reasonably practicable, conducted
fairly and fully recorded, including any comment by the witness and the time taken.
• Dock identification — the witness pointing out the accused in court — is weak and may be insufficient on its
own. See DPP v O'Reilly [1990] 2 IR 415, where an inadequate warning left identification evidence
insufficient.
• Recognition evidence: a witness who already knows the accused may identify them from CCTV, but the basis
of the recognition (how and how well they know the person) must be stated, and the footage preserved with
continuity.
• Avoid contamination: do not show a single suspect’s photograph to a witness before a parade, do not let
witnesses confer, and record any first description before any identification procedure.

8. Corroboration
Corroboration is independent evidence that confirms in some material particular both that the offence was committed
and that the accused committed it. Three categories matter operationally:

• Required by law — a few offences require corroboration as a matter of law (for example perjury). Without
it, the case cannot be left to the jury.
• Warning historically required, now discretionary — for the evidence of accomplices and of complainants
in sexual cases. Section 7 of the Criminal Law (Rape) (Amendment) Act 1990 abolished the mandatory
corroboration warning in sexual cases; whether to warn is now a matter for the trial judge’s discretion in the
particular case.
• Confessions — where a conviction would rest on a confession alone, the s.10 Criminal Procedure Act 1993
warning regime applies. Always seek independent confirmation.
Investigator’s takeaway
Treat every key strand as if it will need independent support. The strongest files do not rest on one witness,
one identification, or one admission — they triangulate, so that no single ruling on the voir dire can collapse
the case.

9. Opinion and Expert Evidence
General rule: a witness gives evidence of what they perceived, not their opinion on it. Two exceptions:

• Lay opinion on everyday matters — speed, approximate age, the identity of a known person, apparent
intoxication, emotional state — where the opinion is simply a compendious way of conveying things actually
perceived.
• Expert opinion — on matters outside ordinary experience: forensic science, ballistics, medicine, valuation,
drug identification and valuation, cell-site analysis, digital forensics. The witness must be qualified in the
field; the field must be sufficiently established and reliable; and the expert’s overriding duty is to the court,
not to the party who calls them. An expert may give an opinion that touches the ultimate issue but must not
usurp the function of the tribunal of fact.
Forensic and statistical caution. Present DNA match probabilities precisely and guard against the prosecutor’s
fallacy — the error of treating the probability of a random match as if it were the probability of innocence. Continuity
of the sample and the integrity of the analytical process are routine and effective defence targets; protect them at the
seizure and submission stages.

10. Character, Bad Character and Similar-Fact Evidence
• Good character — an accused may put their good character in issue; it goes to credibility and, to a limited
extent, to the unlikelihood of offending.
• The shield — s.1 of the Criminal Justice (Evidence) Act 1924 protects an accused who testifies from crossWorking reference - not legal advice - verify before relying. | Page 853

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

examination on previous convictions or bad character. The shield is lost in defined circumstances — for
example, casting imputations on the character of prosecution witnesses, or falsely asserting their own good
character.
People (DPP) v B.K. [2000] 2 IR 199
Holding / principle. Similar-fact / system evidence is admissible only where its probative value — a striking
similarity, a system, the rebuttal of innocent association or of coincidence — outweighs its prejudicial effect. Mere
propensity to commit the type of offence charged is not enough; there must be a real probative connection between
the incidents.
Operational point. In multi-victim or series cases (a repeat burglary MO, a grooming pattern, a distinctive method),
document precisely the features that link the incidents. Those distinctive features are what make the evidence
admissible rather than merely prejudicial.

11. Documentary and Electronic Evidence
11.1 Business and documentary records — CEA 1992
Records compiled in the ordinary course of a business are admissible as evidence of the facts in them under ss.5–6
of the Criminal Evidence Act 1992, on proof of the statutory conditions — commonly established by a certificate
from a responsible person in the business. This is the route for bank records, telecommunications-company records,
retailer till and stock records, medical records, and similar. The court may exclude where the source or compilation
is unreliable, or in the interests of justice. Serve the certificate and records in proper statutory form; a defective
certificate is a standard and often successful technical objection.

11.2 Electronic evidence — authentication and continuity
• CCTV — prove provenance (who downloaded it, from which system, when), keep an unbroken continuity
record, retain the original/master, and note the system’s time settings and any clock offset against real time.
• Mobile devices and messaging — preserve the device, extract under proper authority, hash and document
the extraction, and prove the link between handset, number and user (IMEI, subscriber data, and attribution
evidence such as content only the user would send).
• Communications data — obtain under the applicable statutory regime; the legal basis for access must be
sound and recorded, or the data is exposed to exclusion under §6.
• Best evidence — the old best-evidence rule has been substantially relaxed; copies and downloads are
routinely admitted where authenticity and continuity are proved, but produce the original where it exists and
is put in issue.
Voice and covert recordings (e.g. in domestic-violence files)
A recording made by a party to the conversation (a complainant recording the abuser) is real evidence, not
hearsay, when adduced to prove the words were spoken. Admissibility turns on authentication — who
recorded it, on what device, continuity, and the absence of tampering — and on fairness. Covert recording of
a suspect by the State engages Article 8 ECHR (Bykov v Russia, Grand Chamber, 2009) and must rest on a
proper authorisation under the Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009. Preserve the original file and its
metadata, and exhibit a transcript to the maker. See Volume 4 for the full DV workflow.

12. Privilege and Public Interest Immunity
• Legal professional privilege — legal advice privilege and litigation privilege protect confidential lawyer–
client communications. The privilege belongs to the client and is near-absolute, subject to the crime/fraud
exception (communications in furtherance of crime are not privileged).
• Privilege against self-incrimination — underlies the right to silence and the protections in §5.
• Without-prejudice — communications genuinely aimed at settlement are protected; more often a civil issue,
but watch for it in mixed matters.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Marks v Beyfus (1890) 25 QBD 494
Holding / principle. Informer privilege: the identity of an informant is protected in the public interest, so that the
flow of information is not deterred. The exception is where disclosure is necessary to establish the accused’s
innocence (“innocence at stake”), in which case the privilege must yield.
Operational point. Keep the intelligence-to-evidence divide clean — the sterile corridor (see Volume 10). Source
identity stays in the intelligence system; build the evidential case on independently obtainable material so source
identity is never put in issue at trial.
Public interest immunity (PII). Sensitive material — methods, premises, third-party safety — may be withheld
where the public interest in non-disclosure outweighs the defence’s interest in disclosure. But the disclosure
obligation to the defence is the default, and PII is decided by the court, not by the investigator. Flag sensitive material
to the directing officer; never make a unilateral non-disclosure decision.

13. Previous Statements, Recent Complaint and the Hostile Witness
13.1 The rule against narrative and prior consistent statements
A witness generally cannot bolster their own evidence by proving they said the same thing on an earlier occasion
(the rule against narrative / self-corroboration). Recognised exceptions include rebutting an allegation of recent
fabrication, and recent complaint in sexual cases — evidence that the complainant complained at the first reasonable
opportunity, admissible to show consistency of conduct and, under the modern statutory framework, as relevant to
the issues.

13.2 Prior inconsistent statements and the hostile witness
Where a witness gives evidence inconsistent with a prior statement, ss.3–6 of the Criminal Procedure Act 1865 allow
the inconsistency to be proved, and the court may declare the witness hostile and permit cross-examination by the
party who called them. At common law the prior statement went only to the witness’s credit, not to the truth of its
contents — which is the gap that s.16 of the 2006 Act closes.

13.3 Section 16 Criminal Justice Act 2006 — the resiling witness
For arrestable offences, where a witness is available for cross-examination but refuses to give evidence, denies
making the earlier statement, or gives evidence inconsistent with it, the court may — with leave — admit the prior
statement as evidence of the facts stated in it. The court must be satisfied that:

1. the witness confirms, or it is proved, that they made the statement;
2. the statement was made voluntarily and is reliable; and
3. either it was made on oath or affirmation, or it contains a statutory declaration by the witness that it is true,
or the court is otherwise satisfied that when making it the witness understood the requirement to tell the truth
(frequently achieved by video-recording the statement).
The court will refuse admission where, having regard to all the circumstances and any risk of unfairness to the
accused, the statement ought not in the interests of justice to be admitted, or where admission is unnecessary in light
of other evidence.
Domestic-violence application
Recantation and withdrawal are common in DV. Build the file from the outset so a s.16 application is possible:
take a statement that meets the section’s conditions (statutory declaration of truth or, better, video-recorded),
preserve the 999 audio and body-worn footage, photograph injuries with scale and timestamp, and capture
independent and res-gestae evidence, so the prosecution does not collapse if the complainant resiles. Full DV
evidence workflow: Volume 4.

14. Real Evidence, Continuity and the Chain of Custody

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• On seizure: label, bag and seal the item; record the exhibit number, the finder, the time, the place and the
circumstances of finding.
• Maintain an unbroken continuity record — every movement of the exhibit (to the property store, to Forensic
Science Ireland, to court) signed and dated by each person in the chain. A gap or an unexplained transfer is
the classic continuity attack.
• Preserve exhibit integrity for forensic items: anti-contamination handling, separate packaging, and a cold
chain for biological samples where required.
• Views and demonstrations: the court may view a scene; record the conditions (light, weather, vantage point,
sight lines) so they can be matched to the witness account.

15. Vulnerable Witnesses and Special Measures
Part III of the Criminal Evidence Act 1992 (as amended) and the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017 provide
measures for children and vulnerable witnesses: evidence by live television link, the use of intermediaries to assist
communication, screens, the removal of wigs and gowns, and the admission of a video-recorded statement as
evidence-in-chief for qualifying witnesses. Identify eligibility early, record the witness’s communication needs, and
ensure that any investigative interview which may later serve as evidence-in-chief is conducted and recorded to the
required standard — by a specialist interviewer where applicable. Getting this right at the first interview is usually
irreversible: a flawed first account cannot be re-taken.

16. Proof Without Evidence
• Judicial notice — the court accepts notorious or readily verifiable facts without formal proof (the days of the
week, basic geography, that a particular date fell on a particular day).
• Formal admissions — s.22 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 allows facts not genuinely in dispute to be
formally admitted, removing the need to prove them. Use admissions to streamline the trial and focus it on
the one issue that is actually contested — it strengthens, not weakens, the prosecution case.

18. Defence Will Attack — Evidence-Specific Vectors
Typical attack vectors

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Confession — voluntariness, oppression or inducement, custody-regulation breach, denial or frustration of
solicitor access (Healy / Gormley & White), Judges’ Rules breach, gaps or failures in the recording.
• Search / detention — defective or non-independent warrant (Damache), search exceeding the scope of the
power, no recorded grounds, unlawful detention; then a JC argument to exclude the product.
• Identification — no parade, poor or reconstructed ADVOKATE detail, a suggestive procedure, reliance on
dock ID, poor-quality CCTV.
• Continuity — gaps in the exhibit chain, contamination risk, unexplained or unsigned transfers.
• Documentary — a defective CEA 1992 certificate, unproven provenance of CCTV or device data,
authentication gaps.
• Hearsay — a first account adduced for its truth without an applicable exception; res-gestae conditions not
made out.
• Inferences — safeguards not met (no plain-language explanation, no prior solicitor opportunity, not recorded).

How to bullet-proof
1. Get the legal basis right and record it contemporaneously — warrant route, arrest power, search power,
detention authority, with the grounds.

2. Record everything in real time — notebook, custody record, continuity log, interview recording — so the file
reconstructs the investigation minute by minute.
3. Take ADVOKATE and first-account detail at the scene, in the witness’s words, not in hindsight.
4. Triangulate — never let the case rest on a single witness, identification or admission.
5. Anticipate the resiling witness — meet the s.16 conditions when the statement is taken.
6. Serve documentary evidence in proper statutory form and prove provenance and continuity.
7. Observe the Judges’ Rules and the Custody Regulations to the letter, and let the recording prove it.

How to use this supplement
This deepens Volume 3. It does not restate the section-by-section elements of the Criminal Justice (Public
Order) Act 1994 — those are in Volume 3, Chapters 1–6. It adds the four things that were thin: the real
authority, the cross-cutting concepts proved properly, the group-offence proof problem, and an inner-city
operational playbook, with expanded defence and bullet-proofing throughout.
An honest word on the case law
Irish appellate authority on the 1994 Act is genuinely sparse. The great bulk of public-order litigation is
decided in the District Court and turns on the statutory elements and the quality of the evidence, not on
reported precedent. The operational discipline that follows is therefore: statutory precision plus evidential
rigour. Where English authority is cited below it is persuasive only and clearly marked. Verify every authority
and section number on revisedacts.lawreform.ie, courts.ie, BAILII and vLex Ireland before relying on it in a
live file.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1. Cross-Cutting Concepts — Prove Them, Don’t Assume Them
Three concepts run through almost every public-order charge and are the most common points of failure. None can
be assumed; each must appear in the evidence.

1.1 “Public place”
The Act defines “public place” broadly: it includes any highway and any other place to which the public have access
whether as of right or by permission and whether on payment or otherwise — roads, footpaths, public parks and
recreational areas, cemeteries and churchyards, and trains, buses and other public-transport vehicles. The reach
extends to places the public can access at the material time (a licensed premises during opening hours; a shoppingcentre concourse).

• Prove it. The injured-party or Garda statement should state expressly that the locus was a public place and
why (“O’Connell Street, a public highway to which the public have access as of right”). The charge pleads
the locus “… a public place”. Do not leave the court to infer it.
• Mind the exception. The group offences — riot (s.14), violent disorder (s.15) and affray (s.16) — apply at
any place, public or private. For those, the public-place element is not required; for s.4/s.5/s.6/s.8/s.9 it is.

1.2 “Breach of the peace”
A breach of the peace arises where harm is actually done, or is likely to be done, to a person, or in that person’s
presence to their property, or where a person is put in fear of such harm. The concept underpins s.6 (the intent to
provoke, or recklessness as to, a breach of the peace) and the common-law power.

• Persuasive guidance (English): R v Howell [1982] QB 416 — the classic statement of what a breach of the
peace is. Persuasive only in this jurisdiction.
• The common-law power survives. The power to arrest to prevent an imminent breach of the peace exists
alongside the statutory offences and is distinct from them. Where it is relied on, record the imminence and
the grounds; do not use it as a substitute for a properly evidenced statutory charge.

1.3 Name and address, and arrest — s.24
• On reasonable suspicion that a person is committing or has committed an offence under the Act, a member
may demand the person’s name and address.
• It is a distinct offence to fail to give a name and address, or to give one that is false or misleading; this can
stand as a clean standalone charge (in DPP v Kelly the dismissal of the s.24(3) charge was not even in
controversy — the section is straightforward when the demand and refusal are properly recorded).
• There is a power of arrest without warrant for most offences under the Act. Record the power relied on and
the grounds contemporaneously — the lawfulness of the arrest is the gateway to everything that follows (see
the Rules of Evidence chapter, the exclusionary rule).

2. The Real Irish Authorities
Dillon v DPP [2007] IEHC (High Court, de Valera J)
Holding / principle. Section 3 of the Vagrancy (Ireland) Act 1847 — the old offence of begging in a public place
— was struck down as unconstitutional. An absolute ban on begging in any public place in all circumstances
breached the right to freedom of expression (Article 40.6.1°), and the offence was so vague and arbitrary as to offend
Articles 34.1, 40.1, 40.3 and 40.4.1°. The court accepted that begging may be regulated as to manner and
circumstance, but not banned outright.
Operational point. The vagrancy-era begging power is gone. Begging is now an offence only under the Criminal
Justice (Public Order) Act 2011, and only where accompanied by aggravating conduct — harassment, intimidation,
threats, assault, or obstruction of a person or vehicle. Charge it only where that aggravating conduct is present and
evidenced; mere begging is not an offence.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

DPP v Kelly (with Butler and Doyle) case stated on ss.8 and 9 CJ(PO)A 1994 — verify neutral citation before citing
Holding / principle. Charges under s.8 (failure to comply with a direction) and s.9 (wilful prevention or interruption
of the free passage of a person or vehicle, without lawful authority or reasonable excuse) arising from a protest-type
obstruction. The case turned on (a) whether the statutory prerequisite to a valid s.8 direction had been complied with,
and (b) whether there was adequate evidence that the defendants acted “without lawful authority or reasonable
excuse.” The charges were dismissed for evidential insufficiency on those elements.
Operational point. Two lessons. For s.8, the direction is only as good as its statutory foundation — the member
must have the required suspicion on reasonable grounds, the direction must be clear and lawful, and all of that must
be in evidence. For s.9 (and s.8), “without lawful authority or reasonable excuse” is an element the prosecution must
positively negative once it is in issue — the file must show why the conduct had no lawful authority or reasonable
excuse. Confirm the neutral citation on courts.ie / BAILII before relying on it.

2.1 Constitutional and Convention frame
Where the “disorder” is in substance protest or expression, public-order policing engages Article 40.6.1° of the
Constitution and Articles 10 and 11 ECHR (freedom of expression, peaceful assembly). Restrictions on assembly
must be lawful, pursue a legitimate aim, and be necessary and proportionate. This matters operationally: a s.8 “move
on” direction or an arrest at a protest must be justified by genuine public-order grounds, not the content of the
message, and the grounds must be recorded. Dillon is the domestic marker that expression-adjacent conduct attracts
constitutional protection and cannot be criminalised by a vague, blanket prohibition.

3. The Group Offences — Riot, Violent Disorder, Affray
These are the serious end of the ladder and the hardest to prove. The elements are in Volume 3 Chapter 3; the numbers
and the structure are summarised here only so the proof problem can be addressed.
Offence

Persons

Core elements

Mode / max

Riot (s.14)

12 or more

Present together, using or threatening unlawful violence
for a common purpose; conduct such as would cause a
person of reasonable firmness to fear for their safety

Indictable;
up to 10 yrs

Violent disorder (s.15)

3 or more

Present together at any place (public or private), using
or threatening unlawful violence; conduct taken
together such as would cause a person of reasonable
firmness present to fear for their or another’s safety

Indictable;
up to 10 yrs

Affray (s.16)

2 or more

Using or threatening violence towards each other;
conduct such as would cause a person of reasonable
firmness to fear for their safety

Indictable /
either way

Two statutory features matter on the ground. First, for violent disorder it is immaterial whether the participants act
simultaneously, and no person of reasonable firmness need actually be, or be likely to be, present — the test is
objective and notional. Second, these offences are deemed serious offences for the purposes of the Bail Act 1997,
which affects the bail position of those charged.

3.1 Persuasive authority on “reasonable firmness”
R v Sanchez (1996) — English; persuasive only
Holding / principle. On affray, the violence relied on must be such as would cause a notional bystander of reasonable
firmness to fear for their personal safety. Where the violence was confined to two individuals in an open outdoor
space, with ample room for any bystander to withdraw, an affray conviction was quashed — there was no real risk
to a person of reasonable firmness.
Operational point. Useful for understanding the objective element common to all three offences: it is not enough
that two people fought; the prosecution must show the conduct was such as would frighten a notional bystander of
reasonable firmness. In the inner city the setting usually supplies this (a crowded street, projectiles, weapons) — but
it must be articulated in the evidence, not assumed. English authority; not binding here.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.2 The real challenge — individuation
Collective guilt is not enough. The prosecution must prove that each accused personally used or threatened unlawful
violence. This is where group prosecutions are won or lost, and it is an evidence-gathering problem long before it is
a legal one.

• Dedicated footage — continuous CCTV and body-worn coverage that follows individuals, not just the melee.
Capture faces, clothing and distinguishing features, and the specific violent act of each accused.
• Arrest teams paired with evidence-gatherers — the member who makes the arrest should be able to say
what they saw that person do; a separate evidence-gatherer films and narrates. Keep the two functions distinct
so the arresting member’s account is clean.
• Attribution — link each accused to their acts by clothing/appearance continuity from scene to arrest,
recovered weapons or projectiles, forensic transfer, and injuries caused (medical evidence of the victim, and
of any participant).
• Common purpose (riot) — for s.14, evidence of the shared objective (chants, coordinated movement, a
common target) is required in addition to individual violence.
• Continuity — multi-source footage must be downloaded, hashed, time-synchronised (note clock offsets) and
continuity-logged, or the whole reconstruction is exposed (see the Rules of Evidence chapter, §11).

4. Inner-City Playbook — the Developing Disorder
The recurring DMR North Central scenario: a weekend-night or event-driven disorder on a crowded thoroughfare
(O’Connell Street, Talbot Street, the north inner-city quays), escalating from individual s.6 conduct toward a s.15
violent-disorder situation. The objective is to keep the public safe, de-escalate where possible, and — where it goes
to court — leave a file that individuates and proves.

Before
• Know the flashpoints and the calendar (match days, concerts, bank-holiday weekends). Where the disorder is
foreseeable, plan the Public Order Unit, CCTV operator liaison and evidence-gathering roles in advance.

• Body-worn discipline — activation early and consistently; an unrecorded direction or arrest is a gift to the
defence.

During — the golden hour of evidence
• Command and roles: designate evidence-gatherers separate from the arrest cordon, and a CCTV liaison to
direct city-centre cameras onto individuals and preserve the footage.

• De-escalation first: the s.8 “move on” direction is the lawful tool to disperse before resorting to arrest — but
if it may later found a charge, get the prerequisite right (reasonable grounds, clear direction, recorded), per
Kelly.
• Account for weapons and projectiles — seize, exhibit and attribute. Photograph the scene and any damage;
these support the “reasonable firmness” element.
• Each arresting member fixes in memory and notebook what the specific person did, where, and what they
were wearing — before it blurs.

After
• Stitch the multi-source footage into a single time-synchronised account; produce stills for identification with
continuity.

• Take a “reasonable firmness” witness statement — a member or independent witness who articulates the fear
a notional bystander would feel in that setting (the crowd, the weapons, the missiles).
• Gather medical evidence for injuries; map each injury to an act and an accused where possible.
• Select the charge at the right rung (§7) and, for indictable group offences, build the file to DPP standard with
full individuation.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The single most common reason a public-order group case fails
Not the law — the individuation. A clear video of “a riot” proves a riot happened; it does not prove that the
person in the dock threw the bottle. Win the case at the scene by filming and attributing individual acts, not
the melee.

5. Defences Across the Public-Order Offences
• Lawful authority / reasonable excuse (s.8, s.9) — once raised, the prosecution must positively negative it.
This was decisive in Kelly. The file must show why there was no lawful authority or reasonable excuse for
the conduct.
• Self-defence / defence of others — relevant to s.6, affray and violent disorder; force must be reasonable and
proportionate. Anticipate it where the accused was responding to a perceived threat.
• Intoxication — the public-order violence offences are offences of basic intent; voluntary intoxication is
generally no defence. Involuntary intoxication may be relevant. Do not let “he was too drunk to form intent”
go unchallenged on a basic-intent charge.
• The objective “reasonable firmness” test — the defence will argue there was no realistic risk to a notional
bystander (the Sanchez argument). Meet it with the setting evidence (crowd density, weapons, projectiles,
confined space) and the reasonable-firmness witness.
• Freedom of expression / assembly — where the conduct is protest, expect an Article 10/11 (and Dillonstyle) argument that the policing response was disproportionate. Answer it with recorded, content-neutral
public-order grounds.
• Identification / individuation — in group cases the defence attacks the link between this accused and any
specific violent act. Met only by the individuation work in §3.2.

6. Defence Will Attack — Public-Order Vectors and Bullet-Proofing
Typical attack vectors
• Locus not proved to be a public place (for the offences that require it).
• s.8 direction — the statutory prerequisite not met or not in evidence (Kelly).
• “Without lawful authority or reasonable excuse” not negatived (Kelly).
• s.6 — intent to provoke a breach of the peace, or recklessness, not proved (the words/behaviour evidenced
but not the mental element).
• Group offences — the individuation gap; “reasonable firmness” element unsupported.
• Footage — continuity gaps, time-sync errors, unproven provenance.
• Arrest — power or grounds not recorded; then an exclusionary-rule argument against everything that
followed.

How to bullet-proof
1. Plead and prove the locus as a public place where the offence requires it; state why in the statement.
2. Record any s.8 direction verbatim, with the reasonable grounds and the time, on body-worn and in the
notebook.
3. Positively negative “lawful authority or reasonable excuse” on the face of the file.
4. For s.6, evidence the mental element — the words, the context, and what made a breach of the peace intended
or the obvious risk.
5. Individuate every accused in a group case; secure the reasonable-firmness witness and the setting evidence.
6. Download, hash, time-synchronise and continuity-log all footage; retain masters.
7. Record the arrest power and grounds contemporaneously.

1. Harmful Communications in the DV Context
Modern domestic abuse is conducted as much through the phone as in person — threats, monitoring, image-based
abuse and a relentless stream of controlling messages. Three legislative tools capture it, and all three feed the
coercive-control case at §3.

1.1 Coco’s Law — Harassment, Harmful Communications and Related Offences Act
2020
Commenced 9 February 2021. It created image-based and communication offences and amended the harassment
offence. “Harm” includes psychological harm. A person “intends to cause harm” where they intentionally or
recklessly seriously interfere with the other’s peace and privacy, or cause alarm or distress, and a reasonable person
would realise the act would have that effect. Victims of these offences have anonymity.
Section

Offence

Penalty

s.2

Distributing, publishing, OR threatening to distribute/publish an
intimate image, with intent to cause harm (or reckless). Captures
“sextortion” / threats to share.

Indictment: up to 7 years and/or
unlimited fine. Summary: Class
A fine (€5,000)/12 months

s.3

Recording, distributing or publishing an intimate image without
consent — no intent to cause harm required.

Summary only: Class A fine
(€5,000) and/or 12 months

s.4

Distributing, publishing or sending a threatening or grossly offensive
communication, with intent to cause harm. Once-off or repeated.

Summary: €5,000/6 months.
Indictment: up to 2 years and/or
unlimited fine

• It is irrelevant that the person consented to the image being taken if they did not consent to its distribution;
an intimate relationship between the parties is an aggravating factor on sentence.
• There is a defence for distribution/publication for the purpose of preventing, investigating or prosecuting an
offence.
• “Grossly offensive” (s.4) is undefined and left to the courts — evidence the content and context fully. The
2020 Act also extended the summary-prosecution time limit for certain of these offences (verify the current
limit before relying on it).

1.2 Harassment and stalking
• Harassment — s.10 of the Non-Fatal Offences Against the Person Act 1997, as amended by the 2020 Act,
now reaches communications about a person, not only communications to a person — important where the
abuser targets the victim through third parties or online.
• Stalking — a standalone stalking offence was introduced by the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions)
Act 2023, capturing persistent following, watching, monitoring and communication that causes fear or serious
distress. Confirm the precise section and elements before charging.

1.3 How harmful-communications evidence proves coercive control
The digital trail is usually the best evidence of the “persistent” controlling pattern required by s.39: the volume and
timing of messages and calls; demands and threats; monitoring of movements and contacts; tracking apps or
“stalkerware” on the victim’s phone; and image-based threats. A single message may be a s.4 offence in its own
right; the body of messages, taken together, is the spine of the coercive-control narrative. Preserve it properly (see
§2 and §4).

2. Recorded and Audio Evidence
Recordings are often the most compelling DV evidence — they let the court hear the abuse directly and corroborate
a complainant who may later resile.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

2.1 Victim recordings of the abuser
• Real evidence, not hearsay — a recording made by the victim of what the abuser said is real evidence when
adduced to prove the words were spoken; and the words may be operative in themselves (the threat is the
offence under s.4 or s.5 NFOAP). It is not caught by the hearsay rule when used for that purpose.
• Admissibility turns on authentication and fairness — who recorded it, on what device, when; an unbroken
continuity record; no editing or tampering; and a transcript exhibited to the maker. Establish these and the
recording is generally admissible, subject to the court’s fairness discretion.
The distinction that matters
A victim recording their own abuser is NOT State surveillance. The Criminal Justice (Surveillance) Act 2009
governs covert surveillance by the State and requires authorisations; it does not apply to a private individual
recording a conversation they are party to. So a victim’s recording does not need a surveillance authorisation
— it stands or falls on authentication and fairness. (By contrast, any covert recording by Gardaí must rest on
a proper 2009 Act authorisation — see the Rules of Evidence chapter, §11.) Verify the current position before
relying on it in a contested case.

2.2 999 audio and body-worn footage
• Preserve the 999 call audio — the distressed contemporaneous call is classic res gestae and may be admissible
even if the complainant does not testify (Rules of Evidence chapter, §3).
• Activate and preserve body-worn footage at the scene — the first account, demeanour, injuries and the state
of the home, captured contemporaneously, are powerful and resist later recantation.

2.3 Using recordings to prove the pattern and “serious effect”
Recordings and the message trail evidence both halves of s.39: the persistent controlling/coercive behaviour, and its
serious effect on the victim (fear of violence, or a serious adverse effect on day-to-day activities). Map each recording
and message to a date and to its effect, and the pattern becomes provable rather than merely asserted.

3. Coercive Control in Practice — s.39 and the Real Cases
Section 39 of the Domestic Violence Act 2018 (commenced 1 January 2019) makes it an offence to knowingly and
persistently engage in behaviour that is controlling or coercive, that has a serious effect on a relevant person, and
that a reasonable person would consider likely to have a serious effect. Only conduct on or after 1 January 2019 can
be charged. The maximum penalty on indictment is 5 years (confirm before charging). In practice it is prosecuted
alongside the incident offences — assault, criminal damage, threats, intimidation.
People (DPP) v Kane Court of Appeal, 2023 — verify neutral citation
Holding / facts. The first conviction by a jury under s.39 (Dublin Circuit Criminal Court, convicted November
2020). The accused was sentenced to 12½ years (final 2 suspended) for coercive control together with 12 counts of
assault causing harm (s.3 NFOAP 1997), assault (s.2 NFOAP), intimidation (s.41 Criminal Justice Act 1999) and
attempting to pervert the course of justice. The notional sentence on the coercive-control count was 3½ years. The
Court of Appeal (Kennedy J) dismissed the severity appeal in March 2023, describing a “campaign of fear and
humiliation” that left the victim “fearful, submissive and on edge.”
Operational point. Coercive control is proved by the accumulated pattern across many incidents, not by one event.
Build a dated timeline that ties each assault, threat, message and recording to its controlling effect — that is what
carried the Kane conviction and survived appeal.
People (DPP) v Moody Dublin District Court, 2022
Holding / facts. An early coercive-control prosecution (the subject of the RTÉ documentary “Taking Back Control”),
charged alongside criminal damage, assault causing harm, threats and reckless endangerment — illustrating the
typical “coercive control plus incident offences” charging structure.
A further illustration: a 2021 Galway Circuit Court guilty plea (3 years, with post-release supervision) involved an

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

abuser who controlled where his wife and children could go, who they could see and what they could watch,
alongside threats to harm her, the children and her family — the everyday texture of “controlling or coercive”
behaviour that evidences s.39.

4. Building the DV File — Investigative Substance
The pattern timeline
• Construct a chronological timeline of every incident, message, call, recording, injury and protective-order
term. The coercive-control case lives or dies on the pattern — isolated incidents prove isolated offences, not
s.39.

Preserve the digital and recorded trail
• Seize and lawfully extract devices; preserve messages, call logs, image-based material, and any tracking app
on the victim’s phone; hash and continuity-log the extraction (Rules of Evidence chapter, §11, §14).

• Preserve 999 audio, body-worn footage and any victim recordings, with authentication and transcripts.

Anticipate recantation — build for s.16 CJA 2006
• Withdrawal and recantation are common in DV. Take the complainant’s statement so it meets the s.16
Criminal Justice Act 2006 conditions (statutory declaration of truth or, better, video-recorded), and gather
independent and res-gestae evidence so the case can stand if the complainant resiles (Rules of Evidence
chapter, §13).

Risk, orders and the wider response
• Apply the DV risk assessment; consider the protective-order interface — breach of a safety, barring,
protection or emergency barring order is itself an offence (s.33 DV Act 2018), and harmful communications
will frequently breach a no-contact / non-molestation term (Volume 4, Chapters 3–4).

• Photograph injuries with scale and timestamp; obtain medical evidence; record the victim’s account of the
effect on their daily life (the “serious effect” element).

5. Defence Will Attack — DV Vectors and Bullet-Proofing
Typical attack vectors
• Recantation / withdrawal — the complainant resiles or declines to give evidence.
• “Isolated incidents, not a pattern” — attacking the “persistent” element of s.39.
• “Consensual venting / mutual conflict” — denying the controlling character or the serious effect.
• Recording authentication — challenging who recorded it, continuity, or alleging editing.
• Coco’s Law — disputing intent to cause harm (s.2/s.4), or whether the communication was “grossly
offensive” (s.4).
• Device evidence — attribution (whose phone, whose account) and lawfulness of the extraction.

How to bullet-proof
1. Build the dated pattern timeline; tie each item to its controlling effect.
2. Meet the s.16 CJA 2006 conditions when taking the complainant’s statement; gather independent
corroboration and res-gestae material.
3. Authenticate every recording — device, maker, continuity, transcript; remember a victim’s own recording is
not State surveillance.
4. For Coco’s Law, evidence the intent to cause harm and the content/context in full.
5. Attribute device material to the accused and prove a lawful extraction.
6. Evidence the “serious effect” through the victim’s account, medical evidence and demeanour records.

How to use this supplement
This deepens Volume 5. It does not restate the MDA architecture or the drug schedules — those are in Volume

weight in the inner city: s.15 (possession for sale or supply) and s.15A (the €13,000 threshold offence), plus
the powers, the sentencing position, and the defence attack.
Verify before relying

s.15B prosecution may be instituted except by or with the consent of the DPP.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1. The Possession Spine
Every drugs charge — s.3, s.15, s.15A — is built on possession. Possession in law is knowledge plus control: the
prosecution must prove the accused knew of the presence of the thing and had control of it (personally or through
another). Get the possession foundation right and the rest follows; lose it and the supply charges fall with it.

1.1 Actual, constructive and joint possession
• Actual possession — on the person or in something they physically hold.
• Constructive possession — the drug is elsewhere (a stash, a press, a vehicle) but the accused has the
knowledge and the control. This is the battleground in shared houses and cars: the defence will say the accused
did not know it was there or had no control over it.
• Joint possession — two or more share knowledge and control. Evidence each person’s knowledge and
control — mere presence in the same room as the drugs is not possession.
People (DPP) v Boyle [2010] IECCA 3
Holding / principle. On a charge of possession for sale or supply, you cannot have “possession with intent” without
first proving possession. Mere presence at the scene is not enough; there must be sufficient circumstantial evidence
from which knowledge, control and the intention to supply can be inferred, or evidence of actual
encouragement/assistance for an aiding-and-abetting case. (Where the jury is directed to acquit on simple possession,
that affects the possession-with-intent count.)
Operational point. In shared-premises and multi-accused cases, attribute the drugs to the individual:
fingerprints/DNA on packaging, the location relative to the accused’s belongings, messages, observed handling. Do
not charge everyone present and hope — individuate, exactly as in a group public-order case.

1.2 The s.29 statutory defence — and why it helps the prosecution
Section 29 reverses the usual position on knowledge. Once the prosecution proves the accused had the drug in
possession (or supplied it), the accused is not acquitted merely by proving they did not know what it was — unless
the court (or jury) is satisfied that they neither knew, nor suspected, nor had reason to suspect, that what they had
was a controlled drug. In practice this places a persuasive burden on the accused on the knowledge issue, on the
balance of probabilities. But note the limit: s.29 only engages once possession itself is proved — it does not relieve
the prosecution of proving knowledge of the presence and control of the thing. Section 29(3) lets a defendant rebut
the s.15/s.15A presumption by showing lawful possession under the regulations.

2. The s.15 Supply Presumption
Section 15 criminalises possession of a controlled drug for the purpose of selling or otherwise supplying it. The
intent to supply can be proved directly (observed dealing, messages, admissions) or by the statutory presumption.
Section 15(2) — the quantity presumption
Where it is proved that a person possessed a quantity of a controlled drug that, having regard to all the
circumstances, it is reasonable to assume was not intended for that person’s immediate personal use, the
person is presumed — until the court is satisfied to the contrary — to have had it for the purpose of sale or
supply. The presumption is rebuttable. (Paraphrased; check the exact wording of s.15(2) before pleading.)
Quantity alone is rarely the whole story. Build the supply inference from the surrounding indicia, not weight in
isolation: drugs divided into individual deals; weighing scales with residue; bagging materials; mixing/cutting
agents; cash in small denominations; tick lists or dealing ledgers; multiple or “burner” phones with dealing traffic;
and any surveillance observations of supply. The stronger the indicia, the harder the “personal use” rebuttal.

3. Section 15A — the €13,000 Threshold Offence
Section 15A (inserted by the Criminal Justice Act 1999) is the serious supply charge: possession of one or more

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

controlled drugs for sale or supply where, at any time while in the accused’s possession, the market value (single or
aggregate) amounts to €13,000 or more. It is indictable only, with a maximum of life imprisonment, and carries the
presumptive minimum sentence discussed at §4.

Proving value — and what you do not have to prove
• Value is proved by evidence. Section 15A(3) entitles a member of An Garda Síochána (or a customs officer)
who has knowledge of the unlawful sale or supply of controlled drugs to give evidence of the market value,
single or aggregate. Use a properly qualified valuation witness and document the basis of the valuation.
People (DPP) v Power Court of Criminal Appeal — verify neutral citation
Holding / principle. The prosecution does not have to prove that the accused knew, or ought to have known, that
the value of the drugs reached €13,000. This reflects s.15A(3A): it is not necessary to prove the accused knew the
value amounted to €13,000 or more, or was reckless in that regard.
Operational point. You must prove possession for supply and the value as a fact; you need not prove the accused’s
awareness of the value. So the valuation evidence is for the court, not something the accused’s ignorance can defeat.

• DPP consent — a s.15A (and s.15B) prosecution may be instituted only by or with the consent of the DPP
(s.15A(4)). Build the file to directing-officer / DPP standard from the outset.

4. Sentencing — the Presumptive Minimum and the Recent Shift
Sentencing is not the investigator’s decision, but it shapes how the file is built and what the accused is facing, so the
framework matters.

4.1 The presumptive minimum (s.27 MDA 1977)
• On a first s.15A or s.15B offence, the court must specify a minimum term of not less than 10 years
(s.27(3C)/(3D)), unless satisfied that, by reason of exceptional and specific circumstances, it would be unjust
to impose it. Maximum: life.
• Section 33 of the Criminal Justice Act 2007 tightened this: departure must rest on individual and exceptional
reasons why the interests of justice would not be served by the 10-year term.
• Factors the courts have treated as exceptional/specific include an early guilty plea, material assistance to the
Gardaí, a genuinely peripheral role, and addiction — and where addiction was a substantial factor, s.27 allows
review after half the term, with possible suspension of the remainder. (Confirm the leading sentencing
authorities before citing specific cases.)

4.2 The recent constitutional shift — second/subsequent offences
Ellis v Minister for Justice [2019] 3 IR 511
Holding / principle. The mandatory minimum sentence for a second or subsequent firearms offence was held
unconstitutional as an impermissible interference with the judicial sentencing function (separation of powers).
McManus (challenge to s.27(3F) MDA) High Court, Twomey J, 2021 — verify neutral citation and current status
Holding / principle. Applying Ellis, the High Court held the analogous mandatory minimum for a second or
subsequent s.15A/s.15B offence (s.27(3F)) to be unconstitutional on the same separation-of-powers grounds.
Operational point. This changes the sentencing exposure of repeat s.15A/15B offenders. It does not change the
investigation. Confirm whether the decision was appealed or addressed by legislation before relying on the position;
treat the current state of s.27(3F) as live.

5. Powers — Search, Detention, Arrest
• Search of the person / vehicle — s.23 MDA 1977 gives a power to search a person, and to stop and search
a vehicle, vessel or aircraft, on reasonable suspicion of a drugs offence. The reasonable suspicion must be
genuine and, if challenged, articulable — an unlawful search exposes everything found to exclusion under

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

the modern exclusionary rule (see the Rules of Evidence chapter, §6; Damache; JC).

• Search of premises — by warrant under s.26 MDA 1977. Confirm the warrant route and grounds; dwelling
searches attract the highest protection (Damache).
• Detention — for a drug-trafficking offence, the Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act 1996 permits
detention for up to seven days (with judicial authorisation for the longer extensions). Manage the detention
clock and authorisations precisely.
• Arrest — there is a power of arrest without warrant for a s.15 offence on reasonable suspicion. Record the
power and grounds contemporaneously.

6. Building the Supply Case — Investigative Substance
The indicia of supply
• Product: drugs already divided into individual deals; quantity beyond personal use; multiple drug types.
• Paraphernalia: weighing scales (swab for residue), bagging materials, cutting/mixing agents (e.g.
benzocaine), pressing or wrapping equipment.
• Money: cash quantum and denomination mix; cash inconsistent with lawful income.
• Records: tick lists, ledgers, customer contacts; multiple/burner phones; dealing-pattern messages (preserve
and attribute — see Evidence chapter §11).
• Observation: surveillance of hand-to-hand dealing, frequency and pattern of callers.

Exhibit continuity — the chain the defence attacks
The drugs exhibit moves from seizure → weighing → Forensic Science Ireland analysis → court. Every transfer
must be labelled, sealed, signed and logged. A continuity gap or a packaging/analysis discrepancy is the classic
defence point and can sink an otherwise strong case. Treat the exhibit and its certificate of analysis as the spine of
the file.

Value, cash and the proceeds interface
• Establish aggregate market value early (for the €13,000 threshold) with a qualified valuation witness.
• Where cash and assets indicate profit, consider the proceeds-of-crime / CAB interface and the moneylaundering offence (s.7 CJ(MLTF)A 2010) — cross-ref Volume 5 / Volume 9.

7. Defences and Defence Will Attack
Typical attack vectors
• No possession — no knowledge or no control (constructive/joint possession in a shared house or car); the
Boyle point that presence is not possession.

• Unlawful search — no reasonable suspicion for the s.23 search, or a defective s.26 warrant — then a
JC/Damache exclusion argument against everything found.
• Continuity / analysis — a gap in the exhibit chain, or a challenge to the certificate of analysis or the weighing.
• Value — disputing that the value reached €13,000, or the basis of the aggregate valuation (s.15A).
• Presumption rebuttal — “personal use” to defeat the s.15(2) presumption.
• Section 29 — the accused discharging the persuasive burden that they neither knew nor suspected nor had
reason to suspect.

How to bullet-proof
1. Nail possession first — attribute the drugs to the individual (forensics on packaging, location, messages,
observed handling).

2. Record the s.23 reasonable suspicion (or the s.26 warrant grounds) contemporaneously; keep the search
within the power.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3. Lock the exhibit continuity from seizure to court; preserve the FSI certificate.
4. Prove value with a qualified valuation witness and document the basis (s.15A).
5. Evidence the supply indicia (deals, scales, cash, tick lists, phones), not weight alone, to meet the personaluse rebuttal.
6. Build to DPP-consent standard for s.15A/15B from the start.

production/cultivation/importation, and the specialist offences (not repeated here).

How to use this supplement
This deepens Volume 6. It does not restate the full element-by-element treatment of every offence — that is
in Volume 6, Chapters 1–6. It focuses on the consent framework, the small number of authorities that actually
decide these cases, the child-offence constitutional line, the delay jurisprudence, the procedural protections,
and the investigative substance. The treatment is clinical and operational throughout.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

LIVE LAW — verify the belief-in-consent test before you rely on it
The mental element of rape (belief in consent) is mid-reform. As at late 2025 the law was still the subjective
“honest belief” test (DPP v C O’R [2016]); the objective “reasonable belief” change was removed from the
Criminal Law (Sexual Offences and Human Trafficking) Act 2024 and was being progressed in the Criminal
Law (Sexual Offences, Domestic Violence and International Instruments) Bill 2025 (“Jennie’s Law”). Before
relying on the test in a live file, confirm the current commenced position on irishstatutebook.ie / courts.ie.
Either way, the operational action is the same: record the suspect’s account of belief in consent and, critically,
the steps they took to ascertain it.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1. The Consent Framework
1.1 The statutory definition of consent
The Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2017 put the definition of consent on a statutory footing (amending the
Criminal Law (Rape) (Amendment) Act 1990): a person consents to a sexual act if he or she freely and voluntarily
agrees to engage in that act. Consent is ongoing and may be withdrawn at any time; consent to one act is not consent
to another.

1.2 Where there is no consent
The Act sets out circumstances in which a person does not consent. Operationally, the investigation must identify
and evidence the specific circumstance relied on. The statutory list includes (confirm the exact wording before
pleading):

• the person is asleep or unconscious;
• force, or the threat of force, is used against the person or another;
• the person is incapable of consenting because of the effect of alcohol or another intoxicant;
• the person is unlawfully detained;
• the person is mistaken as to the nature and purpose of the act, or as to the identity of the other person;
• the person is incapable of consenting by reason of a physical or mental disability;
• consent is expressed by a third party, not the person themselves.
Note that the absence of physical resistance, or silence, does not amount to consent.

1.3 The mental element — belief in consent (the live issue)
The People (DPP) v C O’R Supreme Court, 2016 — verify neutral citation
Holding / principle. An honest, though unreasonable, mistaken belief that the complainant was consenting is a
defence to rape; the belief must be genuinely held by the accused. The jury need not accept an account that is
obviously false, and the presence or absence of reasonable grounds for the belief is a matter the jury may weigh in
deciding whether the belief was genuinely held. This is the established subjective test.
Operational point. Record, in the interview, the suspect’s asserted belief and the basis for it. Test it against the
objective circumstances. Whether the law remains subjective or becomes objective (see the reform flag on the cover),
the steps the suspect took to ascertain consent are the decisive material — capture them.
Reform in train. The Law Reform Commission recommended replacing the subjective test with an objective one
(belief in consent must be reasonable, with the jury directed to consider the steps the accused took to ascertain
consent). That change was removed from the 2024 Act and carried into the 2025 “Jennie’s Law” Bill. Treat the test
as live and verify before advising.

1.4 Intoxication of the accused
Self-induced intoxication (alcohol or drugs) is not a defence to rape; the Irish courts have consistently held, as a
matter of public policy, that an accused cannot escape liability on that basis.

2. The Adult Offences
Full elements are in Volume 6, Chapters 1–2; the framework only is summarised here.
