Marital immunity for rape was abolished by s.5 of the 1990 Act — a spouse can be the victim and the accused.

3. Child Sexual Offences
C.C. v Ireland [2006] IESC 33
Holding / principle. The Supreme Court struck down s.1(1) of the Criminal Law Amendment Act 1935 (the old
absolute-liability “statutory rape” offence) as unconstitutional, because it provided no defence of honest mistake as
to the child’s age — exposing a person with a genuine and reasonable belief to conviction without fault. This
produced an immediate legislative response.
Operational point. The result is the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006, which now governs sexual acts with
children and builds in an age-belief defence. The case is the reason the modern child-offence regime is structured as
it is.

3.1 Defilement — the 2006 Act
• s.2 — a sexual act with a child under 15 years.
• s.3 — a sexual act with a child under 17 years.
• The consent of the child is no defence.
• Belief as to age — a defence of belief that the child had attained the relevant age exists, but the 2017 Act
tightened it so that an honest belief alone is no longer sufficient (the reasonableness of the belief / steps taken
are in issue). Confirm the current wording before relying on it.
• Proximity-of-age (“young person”) defence — where the child is aged 15–17 and consents in fact, a defence
may be open where the accused is younger than, or less than two years older than, the child, and was not a
person in authority over the child, and was not in an intimidatory or exploitative relationship with the child.
Terminology
By the Criminal Law (Sexual Offences and Human Trafficking) Act 2024, references to “child pornography”
are to be read as “child sexual abuse material” (CSAM). Use CSAM in reports and statements. The 2017 Act
also created the grooming / sexual-communication-with-a-child offences — see Volume 6, Chapter 6.

4. Delay and Historic Offences
S.H. v DPP [2006] IESC 55
Holding / principle. The test for prohibiting a trial on grounds of delay in a sexual case. The court generally no
longer conducts an inquiry into the reasons for the complainant’s delay in reporting; instead the question is whether
there is a real or serious risk of an unfair trial that cannot be avoided by the trial judge’s rulings and directions. The
mere passage of time does not bar a prosecution.
Operational point. Historic cases are prosecutable. The investigator’s task is to locate and preserve whatever
contemporaneous material survives, to identify any specific prejudice (deceased or unavailable witnesses, lost
records, a destroyed locus) and to address it, so the trial-fairness question can be met.

5. Procedure and Protections

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Anonymity — the complainant’s identity is protected, and (subject to the statutory provisions) so is the
accused’s until conviction. The Criminal Law (Sexual Offences and Human Trafficking) Act 2024 extended
and clarified anonymity. Guard against jigsaw identification in any report or release.
• Sexual-history evidence and separate legal representation — a complainant’s prior sexual history may be
adduced only with the leave of the court, and the complainant has a right to separate legal representation on
such an application (s.3 / s.4A of the 1981 Act, strengthened by the 2024 Act). Flag any such issue to the
prosecution early.
• Counselling / therapy records — disclosure of a complainant’s counselling records is sensitive and courtcontrolled; do not gather or disclose them outside the proper process.
• Special measures — video link, intermediaries, screens, and video-recorded evidence-in-chief for child and
vulnerable complainants (see the Rules of Evidence chapter, §15). A child/vulnerable complainant’s first
investigative interview may become evidence-in-chief — it must be conducted and recorded to the required
standard by a specialist interviewer.
• Corroboration — the corroboration warning in sexual cases is discretionary, not mandatory (s.7 of the 1990
Act); see the Rules of Evidence chapter, §8.

6. Investigative Substance — the Sexual-Offence File
First response — victim-centred and forensically aware
• Engage the Divisional Protective Services Unit (DPSU) early; trauma-informed, victim-centred first contact.
Record the first account / disclosure verbatim with the time and the complainant’s demeanour (recent
complaint and res gestae — Rules of Evidence chapter §13).

• Forensic capture against the clock: facilitate attendance at a Sexual Assault Treatment Unit (SATU) as soon
as possible; preserve clothing, bedding and devices; advise on (but do not pressure) the preservation of
forensic opportunities.

Building the issue that will be contested
• The contested issue is almost always consent (or, in stranger cases, identity). Build to that: evidence the
specific no-consent circumstance (asleep, intoxicated to incapacity, force, etc.) from the complainant’s
account, medical/SATU findings, toxicology, witnesses and digital material.

• Digital evidence cuts both ways — messages, location and app data may establish or negate consent and
contact. Preserve and attribute (Rules of Evidence chapter §11).
• The suspect interview is decisive: secure the account of belief in consent and the steps taken to ascertain it;
lock the Judges’-Rules and solicitor-access position (Rules of Evidence chapter §4).
• Continuity for every forensic exhibit from SATU/scene to Forensic Science Ireland to court (Rules of
Evidence chapter §14).
• Build the file to DPP standard; rape and aggravated sexual assault are tried in the Central Criminal Court.

7. Defences and Defence Will Attack
Typical attack vectors
• Belief in consent — the live test (DPP v C O’R, subject to reform); met by the suspect’s account, the objective
circumstances, and the steps taken.

• Consent in fact — reliance on prior contact, messages, or conduct said to indicate agreement.
• Identity — in stranger cases; met by forensic, CCTV and identification evidence (Rules of Evidence chapter
§7).
• Delay / fair trial — the S.H. v DPP argument in historic cases; met by addressing specific prejudice.
• Reliability / credibility — inconsistencies; met by the contemporaneous first account, demeanour record, and
independent corroboration.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

• Forensic continuity — gaps in the SATU-to-FSI-to-court chain.
• Sexual-history / counselling-record fishing — resisted through the leave requirement and separate legal
representation.

How to bullet-proof
1. Record the first account verbatim, timed, with demeanour; preserve 999 audio and any first-disclosure
witness.

2. Evidence the specific no-consent circumstance, not just “no consent”.
3. In interview, secure the belief-in-consent account and the steps taken to ascertain it.
4. Forensics: SATU early, continuity unbroken, toxicology where intoxication-to-incapacity is in issue.
5. Preserve and attribute digital material on both sides of the consent question.
6. For child cases, prove the offence without relying on the child’s consent, and anticipate the age-belief and
proximity defences.
7. For historic cases, identify and address specific trial-fairness prejudice early.

8. Cross-References
• Volume 6, Chapters 1–6 — full element-by-element treatment of the adult and child offences, grooming,
CSAM, trafficking, FGM (not repeated here).
• Rules of Evidence chapter — recent complaint and res gestae (§3, §13), confessions and solicitor access (§4),
identification (§7), corroboration (§8), digital evidence (§11), continuity (§14), special measures (§15).
• Volume 4 — the domestic-violence interface, where sexual offences occur within an abusive relationship.
End of supplement. The belief-in-consent test is live — confirm the current commenced position, and verify the DPP
v C O’R, C.C. v Ireland and S.H. v DPP citations, before relying on them in a live file.

GARDA INVESTIGATION TECHNIQUES
Volume 1 Supplement — Handling and Possession of Stolen Property
ss. 16–19 Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 · and the Doctrine of Recent Possession

An Garda Síochána — Mountjoy Garda Station

How to use this
This deepens Volume 1. It sets out Part 3 of the 2001 Act — handling (s.17), possession of stolen property
(s.18) and withholding information (s.19) — and the evidential doctrine of recent possession that proves the
guilty mind. Verify every section, penalty and citation against revisedacts.lawreform.ie, courts.ie and BAILII
before relying on it; several recent-possession authorities are old Court of Criminal Appeal cases.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1. The Part 3 Scheme (ss. 16–23)
Part 3 of the Criminal Justice (Theft and Fraud Offences) Act 2001 deals with stolen property and the proceeds of
crime. It replaced the old offence of receiving under the Larceny Acts. Three building-block definitions in s.16 run
through the offences:

• Principal offender (s.16(1)) — the person who actually stole or otherwise unlawfully obtained the property
that is alleged to have been handled or possessed.
• Recklessness (s.16(2)) — a person is reckless if they disregard a substantial risk that the property is stolen —
a risk of such a nature and degree that, given the circumstances of acquisition and the information then
available, disregarding it involves culpability of a high degree. This is a demanding standard, not mere
carelessness.
• Proceeds-of-crime link (s.16(3)) — Part 3 is without prejudice to the money-laundering regime (originally
s.31 CJA 1994, now principally s.7 of the Criminal Justice (Money Laundering and Terrorist Financing) Act
2010). Where the property is the proceeds of crime more broadly, consider money-laundering and the
proceeds-of-crime / CAB route in parallel.

2. Handling Stolen Property — s.17
s.17 — the offence
A person handles stolen property if (otherwise than in the course of the stealing), knowing that it was stolen
or being reckless as to whether it was stolen, the person dishonestly — (a) receives the property, or arranges
to receive it; or (b) undertakes, or assists in, its retention, removal, disposal or realisation by or for the benefit
of another person, or arranges to do so. Maximum penalty on indictment: 10 years and/or a fine — but no
higher than the maximum for the principal (underlying) offence.

2.1 The forms of handling
There are, in substance, several distinct ways to commit the offence — charge the form that fits the conduct:

• Receiving, or arranging to receive, the stolen property.
• Undertaking or assisting in its retention by or for the benefit of another.
• Undertaking or assisting in its removal by or for the benefit of another.
• Undertaking or assisting in its disposal by or for the benefit of another.
• Undertaking or assisting in its realisation (turning it into money or other value) by or for the benefit of another.
• Arranging to do any of the above.

2.2 Two critical limits
“Otherwise than in the course of the stealing.” The handler is not the thief. The thief’s own dealing with the goods
at the time of the theft is not handling; handling targets the person down the chain who deals with the property
afterwards.
The “benefit of another” requirement. For the retention/removal/disposal/realisation forms (but not for simple
receiving), the accused must act by or for the benefit of another person. A person who merely keeps stolen goods for
their own benefit, with no other party, is not caught by those limbs — consider the s.18 possession charge instead.
This distinction decides many charging questions.

2.3 The mental element
Two components. First, dishonesty. Second, the accused must either know the property was stolen or be reckless as
to whether it was (s.16(2) recklessness). Note that Irish law uses “knowing or reckless,” not the “knowing or
believing” formula of the English Theft Act — the recklessness standard is the substantial-risk test, so guard against
treating mere suspicion as enough; it must reach the high-culpability threshold.

2.4 The statutory inference (s.17)
Codified “recent possession” inference

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Where a person is shown to have had the property in possession, or to have undertaken/assisted in its retention,
removal, disposal or realisation (or arranged to), in such circumstances — including purchase at well below
market value — that it is reasonable to conclude the person either knew it was stolen or was reckless, the
person shall be taken to have so known or been so reckless, unless the court or jury is satisfied, on all the
evidence, that there is a reasonable doubt. This codifies the common-law recent-possession inference (§5) into
the section itself.

2.5 Independence of the principal offender; penalty
• s.17(3) — the handler may be tried and convicted whether or not the principal offender (the thief) has been
convicted, or is even amenable to justice. You do not need to have caught the thief.

• Penalty — up to 10 years on indictment and/or a fine, capped so that it cannot exceed the maximum available
for the principal offence.

3. Possession of Stolen Property — s.18
s.18 — the offence
A person who, without lawful authority or excuse, possesses stolen property (otherwise than in the course of
the stealing), knowing that it was stolen or being reckless as to whether it was stolen, is guilty of an offence.
Section 18 is the narrower, fall-back offence. It needs only possession (with the guilty mind), without the “benefit
of another” element of the handling limbs. It is the right charge where a person simply has stolen goods for
themselves. The same facts will frequently support either charge, and the choice is often a DPP direction — in one
case the DPP withdrew a s.17 handling charge and substituted a s.18 possession charge on the same facts (a stolen
car key).
People (AG) v Lawless Court of Criminal Appeal
Holding. On a receiving/possession charge it is not necessary to prove physical possession; it suffices to show the
property was under the accused’s control. The court approved a direction to that effect.
Operational point. Possession is knowledge plus control — a stash, a press, a car boot, or goods held through
another can all be “possession.” Evidence the control, not just proximity.
Possession therefore means knowledge plus control, and may be constructive or joint (see the Rules of Evidence
chapter, §1 and the Drugs Supplement §1 for the same possession analysis). Mere physical custody without the guilty
mind is not enough; nor is mere presence near the goods. Penalty: a term of imprisonment on indictment (commonly
stated as up to 5 years) and/or a fine — confirm the current maximum before charging.

4. Withholding Information — s.19
Section 19 makes it an offence, in certain circumstances, to withhold information regarding stolen property where a
member of An Garda Síochána has reasonable grounds to require an account. It is a useful adjunct where a person
in possession refuses any account. Check the precise terms and any notice requirement of s.19 before relying on it.

5. The Doctrine of Recent Possession
This is the body of case law most specific to possession of stolen goods, and it is the engine that proves the guilty
mind. Stated simply: where a person is found in possession of recently stolen property and offers no explanation, or
an explanation that could not reasonably be true, the tribunal of fact may — but is not obliged to — infer that the
person either stole the property or handled/possessed it knowing or reckless that it was stolen.

5.1 The Irish authorities
The People (AG) v Finnegan Court of Criminal Appeal, 1933
Holding. An early Irish statement of the doctrine. The Court (FitzGibbon J) stressed that the onus of proof remains
on the prosecution throughout — recent possession does not shift the legal burden to the accused; it is a permissible
inference, not a presumption of law.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Operational point. Never plead or run the case as though possession alone proves guilt; it raises an inference the
prosecution must still discharge to the criminal standard.
The People (AG) v Berber and Levey Court of Criminal Appeal
Holding. The Court (Black J) addressed the operation of the doctrine and the proper direction to a jury on the
inference available from unexplained possession of recently stolen goods.
A.G. (Comer) v Shorten
Holding. Davitt P adopted the classic English statement of the direction from R v Schama and Abramovitch — that
unexplained possession of recently stolen goods, absent a credible account, founds the inference, while the burden
stays on the prosecution.

5.2 The limits that decide the case
• It is an inference of fact, not a legal presumption — modern courts treat “recent possession” as common sense,
not a rigid doctrine.

• The onus stays on the prosecution; the accused is entitled to acquittal if there is a reasonable doubt either as
to the possession itself or as to the absence of an innocent explanation.
• “Recent” is relative — the more readily disposable the goods, the shorter the period; for distinctive or highvalue items the inference can survive a longer gap.
• Control suffices for possession (Lawless); physical custody is not required.

5.3 Interaction with the right to silence
The accused is never obliged to explain. But an absent or incredible account is precisely what allows the inference
to be drawn — so the suspect interview is decisive. Where the statutory silence-inference provisions are engaged
(ss.18–19A CJA 1984), observe their safeguards (Rules of Evidence chapter, §5); but note the recent-possession
inference operates independently of those provisions, as an ordinary inference of fact.

6. Proving the Case — Investigative Substance
6.1 Prove the property is stolen — and recently
• Tie the item to a specific theft, burglary or robbery: the injured party’s statement, the reported-stolen record
(PULSE), and unique identifiers — IMEI/serial number, bicycle frame number, vehicle VIN or key code,
asset tags, photographs.

• Fix the timeline — when it was stolen versus when the accused had it — to support “recent.”

6.2 Prove possession or control
• Evidence knowledge and control: where the item was, the accused’s access and connection to that place,
fingerprints/DNA, messages arranging sale, the accused handling or offering it.

6.3 Build the circumstances that raise the inference
• Below-market purchase, no receipt or provenance, cash deal, concealment, a false or shifting account, and —
powerfully — altered identifiers (a re-flashed IMEI, ground-off serial, re-stamped VIN, removed asset tags).

• The interview is decisive: put the property and its origin to the suspect and capture the account of how they
came by it. Absence of a credible explanation is the trigger for both the s.17 statutory inference and the
common-law recent-possession inference.

6.4 Inner-city stolen-goods markets
The recurring property streams are phones (track by IMEI; watch for IMEI re-flashing), pedal and electric bikes
(frame numbers), tools and power equipment, catalytic converters, and vehicles or car keys (relay/keyless theft).
Fencing is often local and fast — move quickly on disposal routes, second-hand outlets and online marketplaces,
and consider the money-laundering / proceeds angle (s.7 CJ(MLTF)A 2010; CAB) where there is a pattern or profit.

7. Charge Selection

Charge

Theft (s.4)

The suspect is the one who took the property

Handler cannot also be the thief for

8. Defence Will Attack — and Bullet-Proofing
Typical attack vectors
• Innocent explanation — a credible account of lawful acquisition (a genuine purchase, a gift, a mistake) that
raises a reasonable doubt.

• No guilty mind — neither knowledge nor recklessness to the high s.16(2) standard (mere suspicion is not
enough).
• Possession/control not proved — mere presence near the goods, or another person’s control.
• Not proved stolen, or not “recent” — gaps in linking the item to a theft, or too long a gap.
• Continuity — a break in the exhibit chain, or a challenge to the identifier evidence.

How to bullet-proof
1. Prove the item is stolen with a specific theft and unique identifiers; fix the timeline for “recent.”
2. Prove control, not just proximity (Lawless); use forensics, location and messages.
3. Document the inference-raising circumstances — price, no provenance, concealment, altered identifiers.
4. Secure the suspect’s account in interview and test it against the objective facts.
5. Keep exhibit continuity unbroken from seizure to court (Rules of Evidence chapter, §14).
6. Pick the right charge — handling vs s.18 possession vs theft — and build to the DPP standard.

GARDA INVESTIGATION TECHNIQUES

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Evidence Supplement — Circumstantial Evidence
How circumstantial cases are built, proved and defended

An Garda Síochána — Mountjoy Garda Station

How to use this
This deepens the Rules of Evidence chapter (§2.1). Most contested inner-city files — robberies, burglaries,
supply, even homicide — are won on circumstantial evidence. This sets out what it is, the proper legal
approach in Irish law, the real authorities, and how to build a circumstantial case that survives the “innocent
explanation” attack. Verify the citations against courts.ie and BAILII before relying on them.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1. What Circumstantial Evidence Is
Direct evidence speaks to a fact in issue without the need for inference — an eyewitness who saw the accused strike
the blow. Circumstantial evidence proves surrounding facts from which the fact in issue is inferred — the accused’s
fingerprint on the weapon, his presence on CCTV approaching the scene, his blood-stained clothing, his lie in
interview about where he was. Circumstantial evidence is not inferior to direct evidence; it is often more reliable,
because objects, records and forensic traces do not misremember or lie, and because a web of independent items is
hard to fabricate.

1.1 Strands in a cord, not links in a chain
The settled image is a cord or cable made of many strands, not a chain. A chain is only as strong as its weakest link
— break one link and it fails. A circumstantial case is a cable: no single strand need carry the whole weight, and the
failure of one strand does not break the case, because the combined strength of the rest may still be sufficient. The
strength lies in the convergence of independent items all pointing the same way.

2. The Proper Approach in Irish Law
The People (DPP) v Cumberton [2020] IECA 136
Holding. The leading modern restatement. The case (the fatal shooting of Michael Barr at the Sunset House,
Summerhill Parade, Dublin 1) was wholly circumstantial. The Court of Appeal reiterated and clarified how
circumstantial evidence is to be approached: the tribunal must assess the evidence as a whole, not strand by strand
in isolation, and convict only if satisfied beyond reasonable doubt that guilt is the only reasonable inference from
the proven facts taken together. Individual strands need not each be proved beyond reasonable doubt; it is the ultimate
inference of guilt that must reach that standard — save that a fact which is an indispensable link in the chain of
reasoning must itself be proved beyond reasonable doubt.
Operational point. Build and present the case as a whole. Map how the strands converge; do not let the file read as
a list of unconnected facts. Identify any single fact that is truly indispensable (e.g. the one piece placing the accused
at the scene) and prove it to the criminal standard.
McGreevy v DPP [1973] 1 All ER 503 (HL, on appeal from Northern Ireland)
Holding. There is no special form of jury direction required in a circumstantial case. The ordinary direction — that
the prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt — suffices; the judge is not obliged, as a separate rule of
law, to direct that the facts must be inconsistent with any rational conclusion other than guilt.
Operational point. The protection for the accused is the criminal standard itself, applied to the whole circumstantial
picture — not a magic formula.
The People (DPP) v Wyse [2020] IECA 190
Holding. Birmingham P confirmed there is no general practice of giving a special warning about circumstantial
evidence; how the evidence is addressed is a matter for the trial judge in the particular case.
The People (DPP) v Nevin [2003] 3 IR 321
Holding. A wholly circumstantial murder conviction (the conviction of Catherine Nevin for the murder of her
husband) upheld by the Court of Criminal Appeal. A central ground concerned the trial judge’s treatment of the
alternative innocent explanations the defence had advanced for the circumstantial evidence — illustrating that the
live battleground in a circumstantial case is the competing innocent hypotheses.
Operational point. Anticipate and, on the evidence, close off the innocent explanations the defence will advance —
before charge, not at trial.
The classic principle (Hodge’s Case, 1838)
Long before the modern restatement, the rule was put thus: to convict on circumstantial evidence the facts
must be not merely consistent with guilt but inconsistent with any reasonable conclusion other than guilt.
Where, on the whole of the evidence, two views are genuinely and reasonably open — one consistent with
guilt and one with innocence — the accused is entitled to the benefit of the doubt and to an acquittal.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3. The Common Strands
A circumstantial case is assembled from independent categories of fact. The more independent the strands, and the
more they converge, the stronger the cable:

• Presence and movement — CCTV (stitched across premises), ANPR, cell-site and handset location,
transport records placing the accused at or near the scene at the material time.
• Forensic transfer — fingerprints, DNA, fibres, glass, gunshot residue, footwear marks linking the accused
to the scene, victim or weapon.
• Recent possession — unexplained possession of recently stolen property or of the proceeds (see the Handling
& Possession Supplement, §5).
• Opportunity and capability — access, means, knowledge of the layout or the victim’s movements.
• Motive — a reason to act; not essential, but a recognised strand of circumstantial evidence (its presence
supports, its genuine absence may assist the defence).
• Post-offence conduct and lies — flight, disposal of evidence, cleaning up, and provable lies. A lie is only
circumstantial evidence of guilt if it is deliberate, material, and explicable only by a consciousness of guilt
rather than by some innocent reason (the lies / Lucas direction) — evidence it carefully, because innocent
people lie for innocent reasons.

4. Building the Circumstantial Case
1. Map the strands early — list every category above that is available, and what each is meant to prove.
2. Secure each strand to evidential standard — continuity for every exhibit and download (Rules of Evidence
chapter, §11, §14); statements that capture the fact, not a conclusion.
3. Identify the indispensable link — the one fact the case cannot survive without — and prove it beyond
reasonable doubt (Cumberton).
4. Stress-test against innocent hypotheses — write down every reasonable innocent explanation and gather the
evidence that excludes it. This is the work that wins the case (Nevin).
5. Put the picture to the suspect — a false or shifting account becomes another strand; a credible one tells you
where the case is weak.
6. Present it as a converging whole in the file — a narrative of how the strands meet, not a bundle of loose facts.

5. Defence Will Attack — and Bullet-Proofing
Typical attack vectors
• The innocent hypothesis — an explanation consistent with the proven facts that the prosecution has not
excluded (the “two views” argument).

• Gaps between strands — inference piled on inference, with no proven fact bridging the gap.
• A weak indispensable link — attacking the single fact the case depends on.
• Continuity / reliability of a strand — CCTV provenance, forensic contamination, cell-site interpretation.
• Lies explained innocently — defeating the consciousness-of-guilt inference.

How to bullet-proof
1. Close off the reasonable innocent explanations on the evidence before charge.
2. Prove the indispensable link to the criminal standard; corroborate it if you can.
3. Keep every strand’s continuity and reliability intact (Rules of Evidence chapter, §11, §14).
4. Evidence lies and post-offence conduct fully, anticipating the innocent-reason explanation.
5. Build the converging-whole narrative; show the tribunal how the cable is woven.

2. Resources — Who to Call
Operational support units
• Public Order Unit (PSU) — planned and spontaneous crowd/disorder management; request early for
foreseeable flashpoints.

• Armed Support Unit (ASU) — firearms or sustained violent threat; mandatory consideration in aggravated
jobs.
• Divisional Protective Services Unit (DPSU) — domestic, sexual and child abuse; specialist interviewing
and victim engagement.
• Roads Policing Unit (RPU) — vehicle interceptions, pursuits, ANPR, checkpoints.
• Garda Dog Unit / Air Support Unit / Water Unit — tracking and searches, aerial support over the disorder,
river/quays incidents.

Investigative and specialist sections
• NBCI — serious and complex crime, including homicide and serial patterns.
• GNDOCB — drugs and organised crime above street level; tie street detections upward.
• GNECB — economic crime, fraud, money-laundering; the proceeds interface.
• GNIB / immigration — immigration status, trafficking indicators, document offences.
• Scenes of Crime / Forensic Science Ireland — scene examination, forensic submission and continuity.
• Garda CCTV / Dublin city-centre camera systems — liaise live to direct cameras onto individuals and
preserve footage with continuity.

Partner and welfare services
• Tusla; HSE / CAMHS — child protection (Children First) and mental-health pathways.
• Homelessness — Dublin Region Homeless Executive, Focus Ireland, Peter McVerry Trust, Threshold
(tenancy).

• Addiction / harm reduction — Merchants Quay Ireland and local drug task forces; divert where appropriate.

• Victims and families — Women’s Aid, Men’s Aid, MABS (debt), the Victims of Crime Office; honour the
Victims’ Charter.

4. Scene and Evidence Discipline — the Golden Hour
• Activate body-worn early and keep it running; an unrecorded direction or arrest is a gift to the defence.
• Separate roles in disorder: an arrest cordon and dedicated evidence-gatherers/spotters; a CCTV liaison.
• Individuate: capture what each person did, where, and what they wore — collective footage proves an event,
not an accused.
• Preserve continuity for every exhibit and every download (hash, time-sync, log); retain masters.
• Record the legal basis — arrest power, search power, the s.8 direction — contemporaneously.

5. Prevention, Welfare and Diversion
Inner-city policing is as much prevention and welfare as detection. Use the tools that keep low-level offenders and
vulnerable people out of the system where the public interest allows.

• Adult Cautioning Scheme and the Garda Youth Diversion Programme for eligible offences — divert rather
than prosecute where criteria are met.
• Problem-oriented, hotspot patrol of known flashpoints; community engagement and visible reassurance.
• Welfare-first for homelessness, addiction and mental-health crisis — refer through the partner pathways
above; criminalise only the conduct, not the condition (Dillon).

jobs.

GARDA INVESTIGATION TECHNIQUES
Quick Operational Case-Law Reference
An Garda Síochána — Mountjoy Garda Station

How to use this
One place to recall what a case stands for and where to read it in full. The landmark cards give the ratio and a
one-line application; the A–Z table gives every authority’s significance and its page. Nothing is repeated —
“full treatment: p.X” points to the detailed discussion. Page numbers refer to this edition; verify a critical
citation against the official sources.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part 1 — Landmark Cards
People (DPP) v J.C.
Ratio. Unconstitutionally obtained evidence: automatic exclusion (Kenny) replaced. Inadvertent / non-culpable
breach may be admitted; deliberate, reckless or grossly negligent breach is presumptively excluded; prosecution
bears the onus.
Apply it. A warrant typo found in good faith may survive; an unexplained dwelling entry will not.
Full treatment: p.30

People (DPP) v Kenny
Ratio. The former absolute rule: evidence from a conscious and deliberate breach of constitutional rights was
excluded save extraordinary circumstances. Superseded by J.C. but the baseline for the modern test.
Apply it. Cited whenever the defence argues the breach was deliberate, not inadvertent.
Full treatment: p.850

Damache v DPP
Ratio. A search warrant must issue from an independent authority; a warrant signed by a member of the investigating
team made the dwelling search unconstitutional.
Apply it. Check every dwelling warrant’s issuing route before you rely on what was found.
Full treatment: p.850

People (AG) v O'Brien
Ratio. Illegally (but not unconstitutionally) obtained evidence is admitted or excluded on a discretionary balance of
competing public interests.
Apply it. The fall-back analysis where the breach is of law, not of a constitutional right.
Full treatment: p.850

People (DPP) v Gormley and White
Ratio. A suspect who has asked for legal advice must not be questioned, nor samples taken, until that advice is
obtained; breach exposes the material to exclusion.
Apply it. No interview questions until the solicitor consultation has actually happened.
Full treatment: p.849

People (DPP) v Healy
Ratio. Conscious denial or frustration of reasonable access to a solicitor renders detention unlawful and admissions
liable to exclusion.
Apply it. Log the solicitor request and the access provided, with times, on the custody record.
Full treatment: p.849

People (AG) v Casey (No. 2)
Ratio. Where a conviction depends wholly or substantially on visual identification, the jury must be warned of the
danger of mistaken — even honest and convincing — identification.
Apply it. Take the ADVOKATE detail at the scene; build corroboration where ID is the only proof.
Full treatment: p.844

Woolmington v DPP
Ratio. The “golden thread”: the prosecution must prove guilt beyond reasonable doubt; the burden on the elements
never shifts to the accused.
Apply it. Build every file to stand on prosecution evidence alone.
Full treatment: p.845

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

People (DPP) v B.K.
Ratio. Similar-fact / system evidence is admissible only where its probative value (a striking pattern) outweighs
prejudice; mere propensity is not enough.
Apply it. In series cases, document the distinctive linking features, not just “he does this sort of thing”.
Full treatment: p.851

People (DPP) v Kane
Ratio. First jury conviction for coercive control (s.39 DV Act 2018); Court of Appeal upheld it — a “campaign of
fear and humiliation” proved by the pattern, not one incident.
Apply it. Build a dated timeline tying each act to its controlling effect.
Full treatment: p.866

People (DPP) v Boyle
Ratio. You cannot have possession-with-intent without first proving possession; mere presence is not possession.
Apply it. Attribute the drugs to the individual — forensics on packaging, location, messages.
Full treatment: p.870

Dillon v DPP
Ratio. Struck down the 1847 begging offence as unconstitutional (free expression / vagueness); begging is now an
offence only under the 2011 Act, with aggravating conduct.
Apply it. Charge begging only where harassment, intimidation or obstruction is present and evidenced.
Full treatment: p.858

C.C. v Ireland
Ratio. Absolute-liability “statutory rape” (no honest-mistake-as-to-age defence) was unconstitutional; produced the
Criminal Law (Sexual Offences) Act 2006.
Apply it. The reason the modern child-offence regime carries an age-belief defence.
Full treatment: p.877

S.H. v DPP
Ratio. Delay in sexual cases: the test is whether there is a real risk of an unfair trial, not the reasons for the
complainant’s delay.
Apply it. Historic cases are prosecutable — locate surviving material and address specific prejudice.
Full treatment: p.877

The People (DPP) v C O'R
Ratio. Belief in consent (rape): an honest, even unreasonable, mistaken belief in consent is a defence — the
established subjective test (reform to an objective test is in train; verify).
Apply it. Record the suspect’s belief AND the steps taken to ascertain consent.
Full treatment: p.874

People (DPP) v Power
Ratio. Section 15A: the prosecution need not prove the accused knew the value reached €13,000 (s.15A(3A)).
Apply it. Prove value with a qualified valuation witness; the accused’s ignorance of value is no answer.
Full treatment: p.868
